records.projectTitle: African Art After Independence, 1957-1977
records.collaborators: Laura De Becker, Kelly Askew, Franc Nunoo-Quarcoo
records.projectDescription: In 1957, Ghana became the first African country to declare independence from its colonizers, spearheading a wave of political uprisings across the continent. By 1970, forty-five of today’s fifty-four African states had regained their independence. The autonomy of these nations coincided with artistic revolutions. Everywhere, artists began rethinking their relationship to the new nation state, to the African continent and to a world fractured by the Cold War. African Art After Independence, 1957-1977 explores how modern African artists were at the forefront of these critical conversations.
records.fundingDuration: U-M funding until August 2020. Additional funding is being sought from the NEA and NEH to support an exhibition and book.

records.projectTitle: Jewish Literature in the Global South
records.projectDescription: Following the Afro-Diasporic tradition of “each one teach one”, the project “Jewish Literature in the Global South” is meant to engage with and raise public awareness of Jewish literature and culture from Africa, Iraq, and India. The final product will be an iteration of a website that includes archival materials; collaborative translations incorporated into the curriculum; and visually rich essays produced by scholars and students The literature examined will be original works written by African Jewish communities. Through the tradition of public readings of novels and recorded oral folklore to men and women unable to read the written word, Judeo-Arabic culture’s tradition of “each one teach one” has embraced equitable access to knowledge for non-readers. As such, African history provides positive lessons to teach the field of Humanities through the incorporation of a tradition of inclusionary teaching and learning practices. Included within the archival collection will be rare testimonies from the experiences of Jews living through the Holocaust in North Africa; philosophical treatises on Judaism’s mission to end racism; folk stories like the 1001 Nights; and histories of Jewish communities throughout the world.

records.projectTitle: Ethnography in Indigenous Shrines in Togo
records.collaborators: Eric J. Montgomery (Michigan State University)
records.projectDescription: This research sought to ascertain the cultural meanings surrounding the gods, practices, and metaphysics of vodu as they relate to daily life in an ethnic Ewe fishing community on the coast of southern Togo. Specifically, we focus on a relatively modern denomination of vodu, Gorovodu (lit: “kola nut vodu”), a spirit possession and medicinal order found among many cultural groups along the Bight of Benin and in the Volta region of contemporary Ghana and Togo. Gorovodu is a faith, moral code, and vibrant philosophy for living. It is also an inclusive system of law and an atikevodu, or medicinal vodu order (lit: “tree root vodu”). Originating as several distinct witch-finding cults in the savanna regions of north Ghana, Gorovodu emerged and coalesced from an enduring history of economic, political, and social relations between coastal groups and those of the interior. The Gorovodu gods spread from the cultural groups of the northern savanna to those of the southern forest belt and coasts throughout the twentieth century through the establishment of shrines built to house and venerate them. As the gods—and the ritual beliefs and practices that surrounded them—embraced local cultural contexts and religious systems, they took on new forms, narratives, and meanings.

records.projectTitle: Heritage’s History in Colonial West Africa: The Archives of A/ONTOM
records.projectDescription: The “Association nationale pour le développement du tourisme outre-mer” (ANTOM) was formed in 1955 to encourage international tourism to the French colonies, including those in West Africa; in 1957, it was renamed ONTOM, “Office national de tourisme outre-mer”. Even though A/ONTOM only existed for five years, its records and activities are important sources for historicizing the notion of “patrimoine” as it was understood in a critical historical period. This ongoing archival research on the A/ONTOM archives and related personal archives focuses on the careers, personal trajectories, and larger social projects of colonial officials and other professionals working for and with A/ONTOM. A focus on the work of both French and African professionals in the field of tourism and heritage preservation during the period leading up to and right after Independence will broaden our understanding of colonial tourism (hitherto understood as focused primarily on wildlife and theorized in terms of empire-building and nationalism) and historicize later attempts at constituting selected socio-cultural practices, discourses, spaces, and material objects as “heritage”.

records.projectTitle: Writing for Publication Support for African Scholars
records.collaborators: I collaborate the University of Michigan African Presidential Scholars (UMAPS). This has been an 11 year collaboration. The names are too numerous to mention.
records.projectDescription: Many different types of research capacity building programs for African scholars have been established over the years. Although many such programs focus on supporting scholars’ efforts to produce high quality research and to establish their own research priorities, the University of Michigan African Presidential Scholars (UMAPS) program is unique in recognizing the additional need for scholars to develop research writing capacities. Writing is central to being a successful scholar. Yet, unlike research skills, writing for research purposes is seldom explicitly taught. The UMAPS Writing for Publication project consists of a writing workshop for UMAPS scholars and a research agenda with three aims. The first, is to explore ways to support the scholars’ understanding of writing for publication genres and subgenres that support a scholarly career. The second aim is to investigate how scholars can develop a more sensitive appreciation of the rhetorical nature of academic writing, and to see how it is shaped by disciplinary and national cultures. This includes how authors establish their stance and engage with readers to create a convincing text. The third aim is to explore how the scholars’ can extend their knowledge of writing for publication with their own graduate students when they return home. By understanding genres that are functional in a particular disciplinary community, the scholars can be prepared to be writing mentors who are able to explain how to craft a text, thus preparing the next generation of scholars to engage in the scholarly activity of their chosen fields.

records.projectTitle: Music Time in Africa Archive
records.collaborators: Kelly Askew, University of Michigan
records.projectDescription: The Music Time in Africa Archive consists of digitized audio recordings and scripts from VOA’s oldest continuously operated radio broadcast to Africa. Musicologist Leo Sarkisian created and programmed the weekly 30-minute international radio broadcasts for over forty years. Begun in 1965 and continuing to this day for a full hour, Music Time in Africa intersperses tracks from contemporary African pop recordings with occasional interviews and excerpts from hundreds of hours of original recordings of traditional African music made by Leo Sarkisian in over 34 African countries. With generous support from the National Endowment for the Humanities, almost 700 radio broadcasts (1996-1996) and associated program scripts are now in digital form and available for exploration and listening. An additional 200 full radio programs (lacking printed scripts) are being prepared for eventual release. A newly launched website for the Music Time in Africa Archive provides ample information about the origins and development of the MTIA Archive. The website also provides direct access to a custom built online platform (click on “Listen”) that supports faceted metadata browsing, full-text search and display of the program scripts, and streaming audio access to the radio programs.
records.fundingSource: External: National Endowment for the Humanities

records.projectTitle: Grassroots Perspective of Central African Theatre and Performing Arts: Multivocality and Social-Political Dynamics
records.collaborators: Joseph Ndundu Kivwila, Institut National des Arts/Kinshasa- D.R. Congo
records.projectDescription: The project originates primarily from my experiences and observations as a theatre director. It does not deal with the political or the democratization process. It rather deals with the contribution of particular artists, writers, and performers to expressing the unspoken feelings of the common people, as well as their critiques of the institutions and individual leaders. After having worked to support the same leaders in their productions, they turned against them, not openly like opposing political leaders do. They turn to the art of the traditional discourse full of innuendoes, rhetorical tropes, as well as the power of rumor, to create works that apparently maintained the same line of social depiction but that were perceived as very critical to the political establishments. This is something that Africanist and drama scholarships have missed in their study of political and social processes of the last three decades as well as the creative works of African dramatists. Performances are of meaningful interest for they remove the political power and process from the official and state institutional dramaturgy to put them on the artistic stage so as to depict and critique their weaknesses in addition to reminding their obligations in front of the common people.

records.projectTitle: Election Related Violence and Security in Africa
records.collaborators: Grant Masterson-Electoral Institute for Sustainable Democracy in Africa; Rod Alence, University of the Witwatersrand, Johannesburg, South Africa
records.projectDescription: Our project leverages election observer missions (EOMs) to assess the degree and kinds of election related violence that occur and the levels of security that are available during national and local elections in Africa. It collates results from roughly 300 polling stations in countries where the Electoral Institute for Sustainable Democracy in Africa (EISA) sends EOMS. Election observers on the ground gather information about security arrangements, incidents of political violence, and public infrastructure and services in the areas served by individual polling stations. The information they collect is geocoded and linked to local election returns. The resulting data sets allow academics and practitioners to analyze the relationship between elections and violence and to identify local factors – such as public infrastructure, security and services – that can inform efforts to promote sustainable peace in Africa.
records.fundingSource: External: Carnegie Corporation of New York

records.projectTitle: The US and the War on Terror in Sahel Sahara: Problems and Prospects
records.projectDescription: This is a project sponsored by the Office of Research at UM-Flint. It examines US policy of "War against Terror" in the Sahel including efforts at providing military assistance to countries such as Mali, Niger, Chad and Mauritania. The research seeks to answer certain pertinent questions: Does military intervention necessarily lead to peace, or other unintended consequences such as national/regional violence and insurgency? What are the motivations for the rise of radical Islamist groups such as Boko Haram in Northern Nigeria? What are the social and political implications of US intervention in Sahel Sahara?

records.projectTitle: Pre-Publication Support Service ("PREPSS") non-profit
records.collaborators: CIRHT
records.projectDescription: Pre-Publication Support Service (PREPSS; PREPSS.org) is a non-profit, that works to address disparities in global health research. PREPSS provides peer review and copy editing services to researchers across the globe who wish to publish their health research in peer-reviewed journals. PREPSS has a twofold mission. First, we aim to increase representation in the academic health sciences literature of authors from underrepresented areas. Second, we work to raise awareness of inequities and social justice in global health research among the next generation of researchers. To achieve the first part of our mission, we assist authors in publishing their research in peer-reviewed journals.
records.fundingSource: PREPSS has many sources of funding - most of our funding comes from the research grants that support our partnering organizations (e.g., Gates).
records.fundingDuration: We aim to be sustainably funded over time.

records.projectTitle: Dictating Letters in Greek and Roman Egypt from a Comparative Perspective
records.projectDescription: This project analyzes the letter writing process in dictated papyrus letters from Greek and Roman Egypt (3rd century B.C.E. – 7th century C.E.). Based on comparison with the process of dictating letters in Mali, West Africa, it reconstructs the various negotiations that took place between author, scribe and others present during the letter-writing event. This reconstruction shows that personal letters, even those containing only greetings, may contain precise information about the location and circumstances under which they were written.
records.fundingSource: U-M: pending ASC (ASRI seed grant). External: pending NIH application for Sexual Violence Research Initiative

records.projectDescription: Excavation of marine tropical fishes from the PETM (56 million years ago), an interval of extremely rapid climatic warming. This interval is sometimes proposed as the best analogue to modern climate change (and biotic and environmental responses to it).

records.projectTitle: Karanis: Multidisciplinary Approaches to an Ancient Egyptian Town
records.collaborators: David Stone (U-M); Laura Motta (U-M)
records.projectDescription: This project, funded by a UM Humanities Collaboratory Development Grant, unites researchers to study the ancient village of Karanis in Egypt from multidisciplinary perspectives. This village, excavated by the University of Michigan between 1924 and 1935 is one of the best documented archaeological sites from the Roman world. Tens of thousands of the finds (artifacts and texts) are present on the University of Michigan campus, as well as thousands of documents, maps, and photographs produced during the excavations. These data have previously been studied in a piecemeal fashion. The Development Grant will permit a holistic approach to data from two structures, C65 and C137. A long-term goal is the creation of the Karanis Research Portal, an online discovery tool containing digitized maps, plans, artifact records, photographs excavation notebooks, and other records in the University’s archives. We envision the Research Portal as a tool that could be used by scholars, by students in undergraduate or graduate classes, and, due to the popularity of both archaeology and ancient Egypt, by the general public.

records.projectTitle: Village Elites Petween Ptolemaic and Roman Rule
records.projectDescription: This project, based on detailed analysis of papyrus documents, describes the various strategies that local elites used to hold on to power in the changing social circumstances of the transition from Ptolemaic to Roman rule in Egypt. It shows how the two parts that made up the village elites in the Ptolemaic period (one “Egyptian,” the other “Greek”) responded in various ways to the new, legally defined, and precisely termed way in which the Romans measured class and status, which was completely different from how these had been measured under Ptolemaic rule.

records.projectDescription: The Global Health Design Initiative (GHDI) is a faculty led program that trains undergraduate engineering students to collaborate with diverse stakeholders to identify and define unmet clinical needs and co-develop preliminary concepts to address health care challenges. Additional program outcomes include approximately 100 student design projects completed at multiple institutions, student-led design-based conference publications, journal articles, and design competition entries, device commercialization, and peer-to-peer mentoring within traditional capstone design courses.

records.collaborators: Multiple collaborators
records.projectDescription: I work to understand how landscapes and ecosystems in Africa have changed over the past 10 million years, particularly in response to climate change and often associated with understanding the the context for human evolution over the past 10 million years. Other elements of my work focus on modern climate, hydrological and ecological processes, with attention to how stable isotopes can be used to track them.

records.projectTitle: Proboscidean Evolution in the Greater Turkana Basin, Kenya
records.collaborators: Meave and Louise Leakey, Turkana Basin Institute (TBI); Ellen Miller, Wake Forest University; Isaiah Nengo, Stony Brook University, TBI; John Kappelman, University of Texas; Fredrick Manthi and Pauline Mbatha, National Museums of Kenya, Craig Feibel, Rutgers University; Thure Cerling, University of Utah; the late Francis Brown, University of Utah
records.projectDescription: I am studying the evolution, phylogeny, paleoecology, biogeography, and morphology of elephants and their relatives from diverse sites in the greater Turkana Basin (including along the Omo River and around Lake Turkana). The taxa involved range in age from late Oligocene to late Pleistocene. The study samples are housed at the National Museums of Kenya in Nairobi (NMK), and at permanent research facilities of the Turkana Basin Institute at Ileret and Turkwel, in northern Kenya. Research methods include comparative morphometric study of teeth, skulls, and postcrania; microwear and isotopic analyses, which are being applied to a rich fossil record documenting numerous lineages of proboscideans, including elephants from their inception until the appearance of the modern African species. I am supervising a colleague from the NMK in her Master's Thesis work at the Turkana University College in Lodwar, Kenya.
records.fundingSource: External: NSF, National Geographic, PBS St. Paul, Turkana Basin Institute; NSF and TBI support pending

records.projectTitle: Writing for Publication Support for African Scholars
records.collaborators: I collaborate the University of Michigan African Presidential Scholars (UMAPS). This has been an 11 year collaboration. The names are too numerous to mention.
records.projectDescription: Many different types of research capacity building programs for African scholars have been established over the years. Although many such programs focus on supporting scholars’ efforts to produce high quality research and to establish their own research priorities, the University of Michigan African Presidential Scholars (UMAPS) program is unique in recognizing the additional need for scholars to develop research writing capacities. Writing is central to being a successful scholar. Yet, unlike research skills, writing for research purposes is seldom explicitly taught. The UMAPS Writing for Publication project consists of a writing workshop for UMAPS scholars and a research agenda with three aims. The first, is to explore ways to support the scholars’ understanding of writing for publication genres and subgenres that support a scholarly career. The second aim is to investigate how scholars can develop a more sensitive appreciation of the rhetorical nature of academic writing, and to see how it is shaped by disciplinary and national cultures. This includes how authors establish their stance and engage with readers to create a convincing text. The third aim is to explore how the scholars’ can extend their knowledge of writing for publication with their own graduate students when they return home. By understanding genres that are functional in a particular disciplinary community, the scholars can be prepared to be writing mentors who are able to explain how to craft a text, thus preparing the next generation of scholars to engage in the scholarly activity of their chosen fields.

records.projectTitle: Assessing Disaster Preparedness among Health Care Workers in Ethiopia
records.collaborators: LemLem Beza
records.projectDescription: There are no documented studies on emergency unit health professionals towards disaster and emergency preparedness at Addis Ababa public hospitals. Therefore, this study will investigate the knowledge, attitudes, and practices of health professionals working at the emergency unit towards disaster and emergency preparedness at Addis Ababa health bureau administration public hospitals. The research will determine whether the emergency unit health professionals are prepared for an emergency/disaster adequately and identify areas that may need improvement in terms of disaster and emergency preparedness. The study will also contribute to the evidence-base for knowledge, attitude, and practice of emergency unit health professionals to disaster and emergency preparedness in Ethiopia.

records.projectTitle: Colorectal Cancer Across the African Diaspora

records.projectDescription: Studies of breast cancer show that the spectrum of tumors subtypes varies across African that has relevance for African Americans and directly. corresponds to the unique pattern of breast cancer. We observe a similar pattern with respect to colorectal cancer. This project will characterize the tumor profile of colorectal cancers from East Africa (Ethiopia), West Africa (Ghana) and African Americans in Detroit. We hypothesize that we will observe a gradient of increasing tumor severity across Africa that corresponds to the more aggressive colorectal cancers observed in African Americans in the US.

RISE offers a training platform for youth employment and entrepreneurship based on new methodologies, curricular innovations and practical training methods.

records.projectTitle: Grassroots Perspective of Central African Theatre and Performing Arts: Multivocality and Social-Political Dynamics
records.collaborators: Joseph Ndundu Kivwila, Institut National des Arts/Kinshasa- D.R. Congo
records.projectDescription: The project originates primarily from my experiences and observations as a theatre director. It does not deal with the political or the democratization process. It rather deals with the contribution of particular artists, writers, and performers to expressing the unspoken feelings of the common people, as well as their critiques of the institutions and individual leaders. After having worked to support the same leaders in their productions, they turned against them, not openly like opposing political leaders do. They turn to the art of the traditional discourse full of innuendoes, rhetorical tropes, as well as the power of rumor, to create works that apparently maintained the same line of social depiction but that were perceived as very critical to the political establishments. This is something that Africanist and drama scholarships have missed in their study of political and social processes of the last three decades as well as the creative works of African dramatists. Performances are of meaningful interest for they remove the political power and process from the official and state institutional dramaturgy to put them on the artistic stage so as to depict and critique their weaknesses in addition to reminding their obligations in front of the common people.

records.projectDescription: The Global Health Design Initiative (GHDI) is a faculty led program that trains undergraduate engineering students to collaborate with diverse stakeholders to identify and define unmet clinical needs and co-develop preliminary concepts to address health care challenges. Over 400 student designers have participated in GHDI curricular and co-curricular opportunities over the last 12 years. records.projectTitle: Screening or Monitoring System for Chronic Diseases in Rural Communities
records.collaborators:
David Burke Professor of Human Genetics, UM Medical School
Elsie Kaufmann Senior Lecturer, Biomedical Engineering, UGhana
Godfrey Mills Lecturer, Computer Engineering, UGhana
Patrick Adjei Neurologist, University of Ghana Medical School
records.projectDescription: Goal: The goal is to provide a reproducible, quantitative assessment of a patient’s medical condition, in places where there is lack of access to basic health care and qualified physicians. We propose to develop a robust technology and communication system that will acquire and analyze high-quality data from a patient using locally-available personnel with modest levels of medical training. The technology will include digital-sensors and computerized data-integration system. We will build a system of interacting, compatible, low-cost sensors and processors to capture and quantify patient health. Impact: Reduction in the level of expertise required for physical examinations will make robust and detailed health knowledge widely accessible. The primary impact of the proposed system will be in monitoring and diagnosing chronic diseases in rural communities as well as developing countries. These areas are experiencing an epidemic of chronic diseases and have few resources to support their patients.

records.projectTitle: Asante Court Music and Verbal Arts in Ghana: The Porcupine and the Gold Stool
records.collaborators: Court musicians, poets, culture experts, and chiefs at Manhyia Palace in Kumase-Ghana. I consult with colleagues in the Heritage and Archaeology Dept, and Dept of Linguistics at the University of Ghana.
records.projectDescription: Although the constant presence of court music and poetry in rituals is often observed, access to the vast resources of the Asante king’s court, including the musical arts and their mediating material culture, has been restricted. In 2009, I became the first ethnomusicologist to be granted unrestricted access to the centuries-old court music and verbal arts practiced at Manhyia Palace in Kumase-Ghana. Historical narratives are combined with detailed analyses of texts performed on drums, ivory trumpets, a cane flute, songs, the poetry of bards (kwadwom), and referential poetry of the constabulary (apae). The focus is on the intersections between lived experience, music, and values, and I refer to a variety of musical examples drawn from court ceremonies, rituals, as well as intimate performances elicited in the course of fieldwork. This book draws on archaeological, archival, historical, ethnographical and analytical sources to craft a view of the Asante experience as manifest in its musical and allied arts.
records.fundingSource: U-M: UMOR, LSA Scholarship/Research Grant, LSA Associate Professor Support Fund, ASC (African Heritage and Humanities), DAAS/SAIO Grant, Office of Diversity, Equity and Inclusion, DAAS Startup Funds
records.fundingDuration: I have completed project. Book is in production and will be published by ending of May 2020

records.projectTitle: Election Related Violence and Security in Africa
records.collaborators: Grant Masterson-Electoral Institute for Sustainable Democracy in Africa; Rod Alence, University of the Witwatersrand, Johannesburg, South Africa
records.projectDescription: Our project leverages election observer missions (EOMs) to assess the degree and kinds of election related violence that occur and the levels of security that are available during national and local elections in Africa. It collates results from roughly 300 polling stations in countries where the Electoral Institute for Sustainable Democracy in Africa (EISA) sends EOMS. Election observers on the ground gather information about security arrangements, incidents of political violence, and public infrastructure and services in the areas served by individual polling stations. The information they collect is geocoded and linked to local election returns. The resulting data sets allow academics and practitioners to analyze the relationship between elections and violence and to identify local factors – such as public infrastructure, security and services – that can inform efforts to promote sustainable peace in Africa.
records.fundingSource: External: Carnegie Corporation of New York

records.projectTitle: Ethnography in Indigenous Shrines in Togo
records.collaborators: Eric J. Montgomery (Michigan State University)
records.projectDescription: This research sought to ascertain the cultural meanings surrounding the gods, practices, and metaphysics of vodu as they relate to daily life in an ethnic Ewe fishing community on the coast of southern Togo. Specifically, we focus on a relatively modern denomination of vodu, Gorovodu (lit: “kola nut vodu”), a spirit possession and medicinal order found among many cultural groups along the Bight of Benin and in the Volta region of contemporary Ghana and Togo. Gorovodu is a faith, moral code, and vibrant philosophy for living. It is also an inclusive system of law and an atikevodu, or medicinal vodu order (lit: “tree root vodu”). Originating as several distinct witch-finding cults in the savanna regions of north Ghana, Gorovodu emerged and coalesced from an enduring history of economic, political, and social relations between coastal groups and those of the interior. The Gorovodu gods spread from the cultural groups of the northern savanna to those of the southern forest belt and coasts throughout the twentieth century through the establishment of shrines built to house and venerate them. As the gods—and the ritual beliefs and practices that surrounded them—embraced local cultural contexts and religious systems, they took on new forms, narratives, and meanings.

records.projectTitle: Ghana Oceanography Summer School
records.collaborators: U-M: Aline Cotel and Adam Simon. We take about 15 US participants per year, including U-M undergraduates, U-M graduate students, an undergraduate from an HBCU, graduate students from other US institutions, and postdocs + professors from other US institutions. Of course we also work with partners from Ghanaian institutions. Our main partners are: University of Ghana: Edem Mahu, Sam Addo, Kwasi Appeaning-Addo, Joseph Ansong; Regional Maritime University: Senam Tsei, Captain Johnson Adjetey, Vice Chancellor Elvis Nyarko
records.projectDescription: The Coastal Ocean Environment Summer School in Ghana (coessing.org) aims to develop capacity in oceanography in Ghana and neighboring nations. We have run the school for one week per year starting in 2015. Over time the school has grown to include lectures, lab work, a field trip to the beach and port, a boat trip, and research projects. We bring about 15 participants per year from the US and Europe, primarily postdocs and professors but also including graduate students and (sometimes) undergraduates. Success stories of the school include enthusiastically attended tutorials on computer programming, which have led to jobs for some of the participants, support of a U-Ghana graduate student, nascent development of a hydrography program, and an Oregon State U MS thesis on the impacts of the school.

records.collaborators: Rockefeller Oteng, University of Michigan
records.projectDescription: WDI’s Healthcare Initiative has collaborated since early 2018 with physicians in Michigan Medicine’s NIH-funded Ghana Emergency Medicine Collaborative (GEMC) to improve emergency department (ED) operations, finances and clinical delivery. The sites include 1,200-bed Komfo Anokye Teaching Hospital (KATH) and 2,200-bed Korle Bu Teaching Hospital (KBTH) in Kumasi and Accra, respectively. records.collaborators: Audrey Bennett, U-M
records.projectDescription: Between automation that excludes humans, and artisans that work exclusively by hand, lies the area of “artisanal cyborgs”: hybrids of tradition and innovation that empower unalienated production. These include the use of digital fabrication, IoT for health, etc. https://generativejustice.org/projects/

records.projectTitle: Automation for the Artisanal Economy
records.collaborators: Audrey Bennett, U-M
records.projectDescription: Cultural traditions in weaving, basketry, and other crafts support skilled labor and more ecologically sustainable ways of life. But they are threatened by competition from factory-made fakes, and increasingly difficult to pass on to the next generation. This research will design, fabricate and test prototypes to facilitate human-machine collaboration in the artisanal fabrication process. It includes AI for textile authentication; cobots in automation; and systems-level platforms for an artisanal economy. While the primary work takes place in Detroit, we continue to create collaborations with our partners in Kumasi, Ntonso and other Ghana locations.

records.projectTitle: Music Time in Africa Archive
records.collaborators: Kelly Askew, University of Michigan
records.projectDescription: The Music Time in Africa Archive consists of digitized audio recordings and scripts from VOA’s oldest continuously operated radio broadcast to Africa. Musicologist Leo Sarkisian created and programmed the weekly 30-minute international radio broadcasts for over forty years. Begun in 1965 and continuing to this day for a full hour, Music Time in Africa intersperses tracks from contemporary African pop recordings with occasional interviews and excerpts from hundreds of hours of original recordings of traditional African music made by Leo Sarkisian in over 34 African countries. With generous support from the National Endowment for the Humanities, almost 700 radio broadcasts (1996-1996) and associated program scripts are now in digital form and available for exploration and listening. An additional 200 full radio programs (lacking printed scripts) are being prepared for eventual release. A newly launched website for the Music Time in Africa Archive provides ample information about the origins and development of the MTIA Archive. The website also provides direct access to a custom built online platform (click on “Listen”) that supports faceted metadata browsing, full-text search and display of the program scripts, and streaming audio access to the radio programs.
records.fundingSource: External: National Endowment for the Humanities

records.projectTitle: Changing the Culture of Disrespect and Abuse in Maternity Care in Kumasi, Ghana
records.collaborators: Veronica Dzomareku (KNUST, 2017 UMAPS alum), recipient of NIH/Fogarty career development grant. Peter Donkor, co-mentor, KNUST
records.projectDescription: This career development award provides coursework and mentored experiences to acquire additional skills for an independent global health research career. The research project will test the feasibility of implementing Respectful Maternal Care Modules (RMC-M) among midwives to change the culture of disrespect and abuse in maternity care in Kumasi, Ghana. records.projectTitle: Creating a Network for Gender-Based Violence Prevention at Universities across Sub-Saharan Africa
records.collaborators: Sarah Compton - UM Department of OB/GYN; Eugene Darteh - University of Cape Coast, Ghana; Ruth Owusu-Antwi - Kwame Nkrumah University of Science and Technology, Ghana; Sibongile Kamusoko - National University of Science and Technology, Zimbabwe; Sinegugu Duma - University of KwaZulu-Natal, South Africa; Akanni Akinyemi - Obafemi Awolowo University, Nigeria
records.projectDescription: Gender-based violence (GBV), including sexual violence, is a serious problem affecting the safety, dignity, health, and well-being of millions of individuals around the globe, with high rates among university students. Rates of GBV in sub-Saharan Africa are higher than in other regions; South Africa has the highest rate of GBV in the world. There is a recognized need for programs that explicitly address GBV among university students in sub-Saharan Africa. However, currently universities are working independently to address this problem. There is a need for a systematic evaluation and a network to share best practices, lessons learned, and resources. This proposal brings together five universities from western (Ghana, Nigeria), eastern (Zimbabwe), and southern (South Africa) parts of sub-Saharan Africa. The team has research, educational, and administrative experience related to GBV programming on university campuses. Thus, the overall objective of this proposal is to create a GBV research and prevention network to conduct initial research on GBV knowledge, attitudes, and behaviors among university students in sub-Saharan Africa. This will help establish a formal Sub-Saharan University GBV Research and Prevention Network to share needs, resources, and best practices to improve the research environment and strengthen individual and institutional research capabilities across network partners as well as expand to other interested institutions.

records.projectTitle: Ubuntu-AI: A Bottom-up Approach to More Democratic and Equitable Training and Outcomes for Machine Learning
records.projectDescription: Artificial Intelligence (AI) can be a threat to creative arts and design, taking data and images without permission or compensation. But with AI becoming a global portal for human knowledge access, anyone resisting inclusion in its data inputs will become invisible to its outputs. This is the AI double bind, in which the threat of exclusion forces us to give up any claims of ownership to our creative endeavors. To address such problems, this project develops an experimental platform designed to return value to those who create it, using a case study on African arts and design. If successful, it will allow African creatives to work with AI instead of against it, creating new opportunities for funding, gaining wider dissemination of their work, and creating a database for machine learning that results in more inclusive knowledge of African arts and design for AI outputs.

records.projectTitle: Heritage’s History in Colonial West Africa: The Archives of A/ONTOM
records.projectDescription: The “Association nationale pour le développement du tourisme outre-mer” (ANTOM) was formed in 1955 to encourage international tourism to the French colonies, including those in West Africa; in 1957, it was renamed ONTOM, “Office national de tourisme outre-mer”. Even though A/ONTOM only existed for five years, its records and activities are important sources for historicizing the notion of “patrimoine” as it was understood in a critical historical period. This ongoing archival research on the A/ONTOM archives and related personal archives focuses on the careers, personal trajectories, and larger social projects of colonial officials and other professionals working for and with A/ONTOM. A focus on the work of both French and African professionals in the field of tourism and heritage preservation during the period leading up to and right after Independence will broaden our understanding of colonial tourism (hitherto understood as focused primarily on wildlife and theorized in terms of empire-building and nationalism) and historicize later attempts at constituting selected socio-cultural practices, discourses, spaces, and material objects as “heritage”.

records.projectDescription: This NIH-funded R01 study is aiming to improve the health of pregnant women, their male partners, and infants in low-resource settings by examining the efficacy and cost-effectiveness of a home- based couples-focused intervention to increase couple HIV testing and counseling and mutual disclosure of HIV serostatus, as well as maternal and infant outcomes in western Kenya. We are conducting a three-arm trial among 1080 pregnant women and partners, randomizing them to home- based couple visits, HIV self-test (HIVST) kits to use with their male partner, or standard care (male partner clinic invitation letters), following couples up to 18 months postpartum. Our theory- based couples intervention has strong potential to increase couple HIV testing and collaboration for family health. Such efforts to engage both members of the couple in support of family health will have important implications for uptake of HIV prevention and treatment. Further, this research has the potential to make important contributions towards reducing some of the most significant public health problems in sub-Saharan Africa, including mother-to-child transmission of HIV; maternal, paternal, and infant mortality; and transmission of HIV within serodiscordant couples.

records.collaborators: Meave and Louise Leakey, Turkana Basin Institute (TBI); Ellen Miller, Wake Forest University; Isaiah Nengo, Stony Brook University, TBI; John Kappelman, University of Texas; Fredrick Manthi and Pauline Mbatha, National Museums of Kenya, Craig Feibel, Rutgers University; Thure Cerling, University of Utah; the late Francis Brown, University of Utah
records.projectDescription: I am studying the evolution, phylogeny, paleoecology, biogeography, and morphology of elephants and their relatives from diverse sites in the greater Turkana Basin (including along the Omo River and around Lake Turkana). The taxa involved range in age from late Oligocene to late Pleistocene. The study samples are housed at the National Museums of Kenya in Nairobi (NMK), and at permanent research facilities of the Turkana Basin Institute at Ileret and Turkwel, in northern Kenya. Research methods include comparative morphometric study of teeth, skulls, and postcrania; microwear and isotopic analyses, which are being applied to a rich fossil record documenting numerous lineages of proboscideans, including elephants from their inception until the appearance of the modern African species. I am supervising a colleague from the NMK in her Master's Thesis work at the Turkana University College in Lodwar, Kenya.

records.collaborators: The REACHE project has generated significant collaborations and intellectual infrastructure, involving multiple research teams including Kieran McNulty, David Fox and Martha Tappan (University of Minnesota); Isaiah Nengo (De Anza College); Ellen Miller (Wake Forest University); Thomas Lehmann (Senckenberg-Forschungsinstitut und Naturmuseum); Nancy Stevens (Miami University Ohio); Emma Mbua and Frederick Manthi (National Museums of Kenya); Robert Kityo and Amon Mugume (Makerere University); James Rossie (SUNY Stony Brook); Mercedes Gutierrez (Tufts University); Susanne Cote (University of Calgary); Daniel Peppe and Steven Driese (Baylor University); Alisa WInkler and Bonnie Jacobs (Southern Methodist University); Alan Deino (Berkeley Geochronology Center); Pierre-Olivier Antoine (Universite Montpellier); Maeva Orliac (Royal Museum for Central Africa); John Kingston and William Sanders (University of Michigan).
records.projectDescription: The goal of this project is to create a multi-disciplinary, multi-national collaboration to fully develop a regional geochronology, paleoecology, paleoenvironment, and biogeography of early Miocene catarrhine and hominoid evolution in East Africa. Over 6 six years, consistent paleoenvironmental and geologic data was collected at multiple early Miocene sites in Kenya and Uganda in order to develop a more detailed model of how the environment may have influenced the early evolution of the oldest apes. Major findings include the realization that the early Miocene was dominate by much higher seasonality than previously recognized, with early apes evolving their signature adaptations in woodland rather than forested environments.

records.projectTitle: Wildlife and Livestock Relationships in East Africa
records.projectDescription: Around the world, the presence of domestic livestock inside protected areas has raised concerns over ecosystem sustainability. Within African rangelands, millions of livestock keeping pastoralists have lived alongside wildlife for millennia. More recently changes in climate, the subdivision of communal land and the rise of new protected area spaces have limited resource access for pastoralists. This has led to a reversion of pastoralists grazing inside both old and new protected areas. Among scholars and practitioners, there are claims and counter-claims over the consequences of these interactions, ranging from competition to co-existence. Part of this confusion has been due to epistemological differences in how we understand and integrate the behavioral, spatial and vegetative interactions and effects within heterogeneous and political landscapes. The purpose of this research is to understand the nature, magnitude and extent of the interactions between wildlife and pastoralists livestock inside rangeland protected areas. Three research objectives are posed: (A) evaluate how different resource management institutions influences patterns of wildlife and livestock resource utilization; (B) determine the nature, magnitude and extent to which resource utilization patterns are influenced by both human and animal agency; (C) assess how wildlife and livestock resource utilization patterns influence the ecology of rangelands under changing institutional political and ecological conditions. To achieve these objectives, research will rely on a mixed methods approaches, combining ecological, social and spatial field methods from a long-term study site within the Mara ecosystem, Kenya. This work will clarify a long-standing debate on the ecological and economic future of rangelands.

records.projectTitle: Youth Educational Experiences and Future Aspirations in Kakuma Refugee Camp, Kenya
records.collaborators: Collaborators at UNHCR Kakuma and schools located in the refugee camp; youth co-researchers are young people living, working, and attending school in Kenya (and some now migrating across East Africa)
records.projectDescription: Education and Belonging in the Context of an Unknowable Future: Youth Aspirations in Kakuma Refugee Camp: Research has demonstrated the significant role of schools in shaping young people’s evolving sense of civic identity, agency, and belonging. Yet we know little about the educational experiences of refugee youth whose opportunities for participation are constrained by encampment and exclusionary citizenship policies. Drawing on participatory and ethnographic methods, this research employs the framework of youth citizenship as a lens into educational interactions in a refugee camp setting. Through participant observation in classrooms and communities, this study documents the educational trajectories of a youth cohort over three years as they develop their future aspirations amidst political turmoil and protracted uncertainty. The participatory dimension of this work explores the ways in which refugee youth leverage their voice to advocate for educational change. As unprecedented numbers of displaced youth migrate and integrate into stable and weak democracies across the globe, it is crucial to better understand how education systems contribute to young people’s prospects for social mobility and belonging. Findings resulting from this study can deepen our understanding of how schools convey, constrain, and create youth civic pathways, so that we can better prepare youth for their roles as citizens in the present and in preparation for their unknowable futures. Currently extending this project; see https://kakumayouth.com/

He is an Assistant Professor of Medicine at the University of Michigan; Patricia Sovitch, M.S.N., F.N.P.-B.C.; Eric Puroll is the Project Manager of Project My Heart Your Heart.; Kevin's role at the University of Michigan Health System (UMHS) has been instrumental in the development of institutional policy and processes as it relates to US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) regulated research at the University of Michigan and beyond; Barbara Medvec, Adviser/Professor, Department of Systems Populations and Leadership

records.projectTitle: Kujenga Uwezo: Promoting Health among Kenyan Gay/Bisexual Men
records.collaborators: Daniel Onyango (NYARWEK LGBTI Coalition in Kenya); Jeffrey Walimbwa (Ishtar MSM in Kenya); Kennedy Olango (MAAYGO-Men Against AIDS Youth Group in Kenya); Vincent Obwanda (PEMA Kenya); Jonah Chinga (GALCK-Gay and Lesbian Coalition of Kenya); Laura Jadwin-Cakmak & Gabriel Johnson (UM School of Public Health)
records.projectDescription: In Kenya, gay and bisexual men and other men who have sex with men (GBMSM) contend with social and structural-level stigma and discrimination. They experience multiple human rights violations such as physical assault, rape and sexual assault by police and state officials, and institutional barriers to housing, education, and employment. This kind of anti-gay violence and oppression has been associated with higher reported levels of psychological distress and poor mental health outcomes. Much of the research regarding GBMSM in Kenya has focused on HIV infection, with a dearth of social science literature directly addressing unmet health and mental health needs. Thus, this study has the following objectives: Objective #1: To explore the range of sexual identity-related stressors and coping mechanisms experienced by GBMSM in Kenya by conducting individual in-depth interviews with 60 GBMSM of various ages living in two major Kenyan cities (Nairobi & Kisumu). Objective #2: To build the capacity of individuals and NGOs that are part of the Kenya National Sexual Orientation Gender Identity and Expression Research Committee in order to conceptualize, conduct, analyze, interpret, and disseminate social science research data using community-based participatory research (CBPR).
records.fundingSource: U-M: School of Public Health Office of Global Public Health
records.fundingDuration: Our funding has expired, but we have kept the project going through volunteer research assistants

records.projectTitle: Randomized Controlled Trial of the Shauriana Intervention to Integrate PrEP, Sexual Health, and Mental Health Support for Gay, Bisexual, and Other Men Who Have Sex with Men in Kenya
records.collaborators: Wilson Odero, M.D., Ph.D. (Maseno University in Kenya); Susan Graham, M.D., Ph.D. (University of Washington); Daniel Peter Onyango (NYARWEK LGBTI Network in Kenya); Fredrick Otieno, M.D. (Nyanza Reproductive Health Society in Kenya); Bob Bailey, Ph.D. (University of Illinois at Chicago); Rivet Amico, Ph.D. and Laura Jadwin-Cakmak, MPH (UM School of Public Health)
records.projectDescription: Gay, bisexual, and other men who have sex with men (GBMSM) are at high risk for HIV-1 acquisition, especially in rights-constrained settings such as Kenya, where men’s access to HIV prevention has been impeded by homophobia, stigma, and discrimination. Pre-exposure prophylaxis (PrEP) has the potential to greatly reduce HIV acquisition risk in this key population if accessible, sustainable PrEP programming with tailored, effective adherence support can be provided. To this end, close collaboration between PrEP program implementers and GBMSM-led community-based organizations is essential. We have recently completed a project to identify the multi-level barriers and facilitators that influence GBMSM’s ability to engage in the PrEP continuum of care through in-depth interviews with 20 peer navigators working to promote HIV testing and PrEP in the community and with 40 GBMSM with different PrEP experiences. Based on this work, we have adapted and enhanced a theory-based and culturally relevant PrEP support intervention that we named Shauriana (“we counsel each other”). The Shauriana intervention merges peer navigation and “integrated Next Step Counseling” (iNSC) to promote sexual health protection strategies, including PrEP uptake and adherence, among GBMSM in Kisumu. The present proposal aims to pilot the intervention among 10 participants for 3 months, and then to evaluate the Shauriana intervention for acceptability, feasibility, safety, and estimated effect on PrEP uptake and adherence, compared to standard of care, in a small randomized, controlled study with 60 participants followed for 6 months after enrollment. We anticipate that results of the proposed research will have high impact by ensuring GBMSM involvement in the scale-up of effective PrEP programming for this key population in Kenya and providing a peer-led PrEP support model for GBMSM and other vulnerable and marginalized populations in African settings.
records.fundingSource: External: National Institute of Mental Health

records.projectTitle: WEMA: Improving the Mental Health and Well-being of LGBTI Adults in Kenya
records.collaborators: Gabriel Johnson, MPH (UM-SPH); Laura Jadwin-Cakmak (UM-SPH); Hyuri McDowell (UM-SPH); Caroline Rucah (NYARWEK LGBTI Coalition in Kenya); Cecil Conchela (NYARWEK); Daniel Peter Onyango (NYARWEK)
records.projectDescription: The lesbian, gay, bisexual, transgender and intersex (LGBTI) community in Kenya has voiced the need for resources to improve the mental health and well-being of its members. LGBTI persons often encounter stigma, stress, discrimination, and marginalization because of their sexual orientation and/or gender identity and expression, leading to elevated risk for depression and substance use disorders (WHO, 2018). Together members of UM School of Public Health and NYARWEK LGBTI Coalition in Western Kenya worked together over a two year period to develop WEMA. WEMA is a manualized intervention consisting of six structured sessions delivered by two trained facilitators, with groups of 6-10 LGBTI adults. The goal of this trauma-informed, empowerment-based program is to empower LGBTI community members in Kenya to improve their overall mental health and well-being. WEMA is informed by the Minority Stress Theory (Meyer, 1995), which posits that negative mental health outcomes seen among LGBTI populations are due to increased stress and discrimination and lower levels of social support due to one’s LGBTI identity. It also incorporates Critical Consciousness (Freire, 1970), a sociopolitical educative approach that helps participants recognize the impact of structural and internalized oppression. Intervention activities incorporate techniques from Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (Bandura, 1986). We have conducted a small pilot of WEMA and are seeking resources to both provide the program to the community and to conduct a large-scale trial of the intervention.

records.projectTitle: Writing for Publication Support for African Scholars
records.collaborators: I collaborate the University of Michigan African Presidential Scholars (UMAPS). This has been an 11 year collaboration. The names are too numerous to mention.
records.projectDescription: Many different types of research capacity building programs for African scholars have been established over the years. Although many such programs focus on supporting scholars’ efforts to produce high quality research and to establish their own research priorities, the University of Michigan African Presidential Scholars (UMAPS) program is unique in recognizing the additional need for scholars to develop research writing capacities. Writing is central to being a successful scholar. Yet, unlike research skills, writing for research purposes is seldom explicitly taught. The UMAPS Writing for Publication project consists of a writing workshop for UMAPS scholars and a research agenda with three aims. The first, is to explore ways to support the scholars’ understanding of writing for publication genres and subgenres that support a scholarly career. The second aim is to investigate how scholars can develop a more sensitive appreciation of the rhetorical nature of academic writing, and to see how it is shaped by disciplinary and national cultures. This includes how authors establish their stance and engage with readers to create a convincing text. The third aim is to explore how the scholars’ can extend their knowledge of writing for publication with their own graduate students when they return home. By understanding genres that are functional in a particular disciplinary community, the scholars can be prepared to be writing mentors who are able to explain how to craft a text, thus preparing the next generation of scholars to engage in the scholarly activity of their chosen fields.

records.projectTitle: Music Time in Africa Archive
records.collaborators: Kelly Askew, University of Michigan
records.projectDescription: The Music Time in Africa Archive consists of digitized audio recordings and scripts from VOA’s oldest continuously operated radio broadcast to Africa. Musicologist Leo Sarkisian created and programmed the weekly 30-minute international radio broadcasts for over forty years. Begun in 1965 and continuing to this day for a full hour, Music Time in Africa intersperses tracks from contemporary African pop recordings with occasional interviews and excerpts from hundreds of hours of original recordings of traditional African music made by Leo Sarkisian in over 34 African countries. With generous support from the National Endowment for the Humanities, almost 700 radio broadcasts (1996-1996) and associated program scripts are now in digital form and available for exploration and listening. An additional 200 full radio programs (lacking printed scripts) are being prepared for eventual release. A newly launched website for the Music Time in Africa Archive provides ample information about the origins and development of the MTIA Archive. The website also provides direct access to a custom built online platform (click on “Listen”) that supports faceted metadata browsing, full-text search and display of the program scripts, and streaming audio access to the radio programs.
records.fundingSource: External: National Endowment for the Humanities

records.projectTitle: Edible Insects to Address Food Insecurity at Maternity Waiting Homes in Liberia
records.collaborators: Cheryl Moyer - UM, School of Medicine; Jacob Anakware - Founder and CEO; AnePaare Farms & University of Energy and Natural Resources, Ghana (former Partner II Fellow); Alphonso Kofa - Bong County Health Department, Liberia; Aloysius Nyanplu - Bong County Health Department, Liberia; Barsee Zogbaye - Bong County Health Department
records.projectDescription: In Liberia, food insecurity is widespread, with every fifth household in the country considered food insecure. In poor rural areas, the proportion of food insecure households is significantly higher, with estimates suggesting as many as 80% are food insecure. The goal of this project is to train local midwives and traditional birth attendants at rural health facilities in Liberia to cultivate edible insects that can be consumed by the pregnant women staying at maternity waiting homes (MWHs). In addition, we will evaluate the cultivating of edible insects as an income generating activity (IGA) to provide a sustainable source of revenue at rural MWHs.
records.fundingSource: U-M: Global REACH - confirmed; Graham Institute - pending

records.projectTitle: Jewish Literature in the Global South
records.projectDescription: Following the Afro-Diasporic tradition of “each one teach one”, the project “Jewish Literature in the Global South” is meant to engage with and raise public awareness of Jewish literature and culture from Africa, Iraq, and India. The final product will be an iteration of a website that includes archival materials; collaborative translations incorporated into the curriculum; and visually rich essays produced by scholars and students The literature examined will be original works written by African Jewish communities. Through the tradition of public readings of novels and recorded oral folklore to men and women unable to read the written word, Judeo-Arabic culture’s tradition of “each one teach one” has embraced equitable access to knowledge for non-readers. As such, African history provides positive lessons to teach the field of Humanities through the incorporation of a tradition of inclusionary teaching and learning practices. Included within the archival collection will be rare testimonies from the experiences of Jews living through the Holocaust in North Africa; philosophical treatises on Judaism’s mission to end racism; folk stories like the 1001 Nights; and histories of Jewish communities throughout the world.

The program will equip young people in the U.S. and MENA region with the competencies they need to communicate, problem-solve and collaborate in a global team environment—all essential 21st century skills in an interconnected world.

records.projectTitle: CNH-L: Energy Transitions and Environmental Change in East and Southern Africa's Coupled Human, Terrestrial, and Atmospheric Systems
records.collaborators: Robert Bailis (SEI), Andrew Grieshop (NCSU), Jason West (UNC-CH), Jared Bowden (UNC-CH), Adrian Ghilardi (UNAM), Charles Jumbe (LUANAR), Thabbie Chilongo (LUANAR), Ther Aung (UNC-CH)
records.projectDescription: We leverage dynamism in East and Southern Africa's coupled human, terrestrial and atmospheric systems and its implications for environmental change to predict past, present and future impacts on regional climate, land use, and air quality. We highlight energy transitions as we hypothesize that biomass use and associated dynamics of coupled human, terrestrial, and atmospheric systems play a major role in regional environmental change. Our proposed study has three main scientific challenges. First we seek to understand the human drivers of change in land use and air quality at relatively fine spatial and temporal scales. Second, we use high quality field data from Malawi to calibrate, validate, and scale regional land use and air quality models to understand past, present and future scenarios of environmental change in the region. Third, we evaluate the potential for a novel scalable household energy intervention to mitigate potential impacts of environmental change such as forest loss and deteriorating air quality. Our project will improve capabilities for predicting system responses to future changes, including appropriate estimates of undertraining in modelling predictions.

records.projectTitle: PIRE: Confronting Energy Poverty: Building an Interdisciplinary Evidence Base, Network, and Capacity for Transformative Change
records.collaborators: Barbara Entwisle , UNC-Chapel Hill; Michael Emch, UNC-Chapel Hill; Stephen Kelley, North Carolina State University; Andrew Grieshop, North Carolina State University
records.projectDescription: Sub-Saharan Africa is the epicenter of the global challenge of energy poverty, defined as the lack of access to electricity and reliance on biomass fuels. In Southern Africa, the absolute number of energy poor is projected to increase through 2050. Energy poverty has implications for climate, environmental sustainability, human health, and well-being, with negative impacts realized at individual and collective-scales, and in local, regional, and global contexts. The complex socio-environmental challenge of energy poverty requires contributions from engineering and the natural and social sciences, with integration of evidence and learning using robust interdisciplinary frameworks. Through the Energy Poverty PIRE (EPP) we will foster and sustain a network of academic, practitioner, and policy communities to fill critical gaps in the theoretical and empirical evidence base regarding strategies for mitigating energy poverty. International partnership is critical to the identification and study of important novel and scalable energy poverty innovations, analyzing the socio-environmental causes and consequences of energy poverty at multiple-scales, and to developing a virtuous cycle of evidence based policy making. The EPP will train over 70 postdoctoral, graduate and undergraduate researchers from a disciplines as diverse as anthropology and engineering in excellence in interdisciplinary collaborative research in low resource international settings

records.projectTitle: Total Market Approach to the Last Mile
records.projectDescription: In this project, funded by the Reproductive Health Supplies Coalition, WDI’s Healthcare team conducted a contraceptives distributor landscape analysis and delivered actionable and stakeholder receptivity-tested concepts for stimulating the private sector provision of family planning products and services in rural, remote and other underserved populations.

records.projectTitle: Scheduling Conflict: Effects of Overlap in the School and Farming Calendars on Education
records.projectDescription: How does overlap in the school and farming calendars affect children's educational outcomes? This paper examines how a change in the school calendar – that shifted school days to a time of peak agricultural labor demand – affected educational outcomes in Malawi. Using panel household data, I construct two measures predicting how children were affected by both household and local farm labor demand, based on each household’s pre-policy crop production and FAO-recommended crop-specific dates for sowing and harvest. Children in areas where the average household faced increased calendar overlap between school days and farming activities saw substantially lower grade progression from before to after the school calendar change, compared to areas where households faced less calendar overlap. A one standard deviation increase in local-average overlap (5.7 days) leads to 0.34 fewer years of grade progression over seven years. This study highlights how a seemingly innocuous administrative policy can increase the opportunity cost of schooling and negatively affect schooling investments.

records.projectTitle: Dictating Letters in Greek and Roman Egypt from a Comparative Perspective
records.projectDescription: This project analyzes the letter writing process in dictated papyrus letters from Greek and Roman Egypt (3rd century B.C.E. – 7th century C.E.). Based on comparison with the process of dictating letters in Mali, West Africa, it reconstructs the various negotiations that took place between author, scribe and others present during the letter-writing event. This reconstruction shows that personal letters, even those containing only greetings, may contain precise information about the location and circumstances under which they were written.
records.fundingSource: U-M: pending ASC (ASRI seed grant). External: pending NIH application for Sexual Violence Research Initiative

records.projectTitle: Heritage’s History in Colonial West Africa: The Archives of A/ONTOM
records.projectDescription: The “Association nationale pour le développement du tourisme outre-mer” (ANTOM) was formed in 1955 to encourage international tourism to the French colonies, including those in West Africa; in 1957, it was renamed ONTOM, “Office national de tourisme outre-mer”. Even though A/ONTOM only existed for five years, its records and activities are important sources for historicizing the notion of “patrimoine” as it was understood in a critical historical period. This ongoing archival research on the A/ONTOM archives and related personal archives focuses on the careers, personal trajectories, and larger social projects of colonial officials and other professionals working for and with A/ONTOM. A focus on the work of both French and African professionals in the field of tourism and heritage preservation during the period leading up to and right after Independence will broaden our understanding of colonial tourism (hitherto understood as focused primarily on wildlife and theorized in terms of empire-building and nationalism) and historicize later attempts at constituting selected socio-cultural practices, discourses, spaces, and material objects as “heritage”.

records.projectTitle: Election Related Violence and Security in Africa
records.collaborators: Grant Masterson-Electoral Institute for Sustainable Democracy in Africa; Rod Alence, University of the Witwatersrand, Johannesburg, South Africa
records.projectDescription: Our project leverages election observer missions (EOMs) to assess the degree and kinds of election related violence that occur and the levels of security that are available during national and local elections in Africa. It collates results from roughly 300 polling stations in countries where the Electoral Institute for Sustainable Democracy in Africa (EISA) sends EOMS. Election observers on the ground gather information about security arrangements, incidents of political violence, and public infrastructure and services in the areas served by individual polling stations. The information they collect is geocoded and linked to local election returns. The resulting data sets allow academics and practitioners to analyze the relationship between elections and violence and to identify local factors – such as public infrastructure, security and services – that can inform efforts to promote sustainable peace in Africa.
records.fundingSource: External: Carnegie Corporation of New York

records.projectTitle: Social Stigma as a Barrier to HIV Testing - Evidence from a Randomized Experiment in Mozambique
records.projectDescription: Public health experts have seen the stigma as a leading barrier affecting the delivery of HIV-related health care. By using a field experiment in Mozambique, this study identifies the role of stigma concerns in hindering HIV testing and quantifies the stigma barrier. To obtain local measures of the HIV stigma environment in the study sites, we conducted a baseline survey one year before the experiment. Experiment participants with excessive concerns, defined as overestimating the stigma in their communities, were randomly assigned an intervention to relieve stigma concerns. The intervention, which drew upon findings from the baseline survey, was designed to reveal the correct degree of stigma that a participant had overestimated. Analyses show that this intervention raised the HIV test uptake rate by 7.7 percentage points (or by 37 percent) from 20.7 percent under the control condition. To quantify the intervention effect, I introduced testing coupons of different values to estimate the demand curve for an HIV test. The concern-relieving intervention raised an individual’s willingness-to-pay for an HIV test by $1.30, or more than half of the daily cost-of-living in the study population.
records.fundingSource: U-M: MITRE Research fund, department of economics; Rackham International Research Grant
