records.projectTitle: Improving the Efficient Use of National Resources: A Cost-Effectiveness Analysis (CEA) Exercise to Guide the Ministry of Health’s Decisions in Mozambique
records.projectDescription: The project responds to a direct request from the National Institute of Health (INS) at the Ministry of Health (MOH) in Mozambique. Malaria-endemic countries such as Mozambique have to decide how much of their limited resources allocate towards national policies. The objective of this project is to support Dr. Eduardo Samo Gudo, Scientific Director at the INS to conduct a cost-effectiveness analysis (CEA) of the existing malaria control programmes. These policies include the distribution of Long-Lasting Insecticidal Nets (LLINs), indoor residual spraying (IRS), Intermittent Preventive Treatment (IPT) for pregnant mothers and children, and integrated Community Case Management (iCCM), including testing and treatment, delivered through Community Health Workers (Agentes Polivalentes Elementares APEs). By evaluating and comparing their costs and health benefits, the analysis will determine the value of each intervention for the INS to invest in, and it will provide evidence on which programmes are resourceful and sustainable solutions for controlling malaria in the country. By using an evidence-based approach to determine funding allocation towards various malaria control strategies, the government will increase its capacity to spend resources more effectively and it will take steps towards improving the economic growth of Mozambique. The project consists of two phases: (1) Gathering data on costs and health benefits. The scope is to gather, on the one hand, data on financial and opportunity costs, and, on the other hand, data on the number of malaria cases and information necessary to construct common measurements of disease burden such as Disability-Adjusted Life Years (DALYs). (2) Conducting a cost-effectiveness analysis by comparing several policies implemented (LLITNs, IRS, IPT, the use of APEs to provide health promotion, testing and treatment or iCCM) in the country. The analysis will calculate the Incremental Cost Effectiveness Ratio (ICER) which defines the relative difference in cost associated with one strategy versus the difference in effectiveness of the competing alternative. By taking a governmental perspective, the CEA aims at understanding which policies could have the most widespread impact on health outcomes and it will inform policy makers on how to allocate investments across programmes.

records.projectTitle: Health and Educational Decision-Making in the Aftermath of a Disaster: Evidence from Cyclone Idai in Mozambique
records.collaborators: Dean Yang, James Riddell IV, Hang Yu, James Allen IV (University of Michigan), Arlete Mahumane (Beira Operational Research Center - CIOB)
records.projectDescription: We study how disasters affect health and educational decisions, how changes in economic preference parameters mediate these effects, and the protective role of community-based education and health interventions. We are conducting a randomized controlled trial of an education and health program, Força à Comunidade e Crianças (FCC), with a study sample across three Mozambican provinces who were surveyed and randomly assigned to program exposure in 2017-18. The program includes a village savings and loan association (VSLA) component, which may improve disaster resilience, as well as student educational subsidies, which may also help children maintain school enrollment in the wake of a disaster. In March 2019, our study areas were struck (to varying degrees) by Cyclone Idai, the most destructive cyclone ever recorded in Africa. Substantial shares of study households were displaced to refugee camps. We propose intensive current follow-up and later re-surveys of study households to understand short- and medium-term disaster impacts, disaster-induced changes in preferences (e.g., present bias, risk aversion), and whether the education and health program improves post-disaster resilience.
records.fundingSource: External: Dubai Cares Evidence for Education in Emergencies (E-Cubed)

records.projectTitle: Health, Education, and Economic Interventions for Orphans and Vulnerable Children
records.collaborators: James Riddell (University of Michigan Medial School) Arlete Mahumane (Beira Operational Research Center, National Institute of Health, Mozambique) Hang Yu (Department of Economics, University of Michigan) James Allen (Department of Economics, University of Michigan)
records.projectDescription: We report the results of a randomized controlled trial of a U.S. government-funded program in Mozambique to help families and communities cope with HIV/AIDS. We hypothesize that the program raises HIV testing rates by improving HIV-related information and reducing stigma concerns. Primary outcomes are survey-reported and directly-observed HIV testing rates. We also examine secondary outcomes related to information and stigma mechanisms, and secondary outcomes such as school attendance and household well-being. Causal identification exploits multilevel random assignment (of communities to program receipt, and of treatment-community households to encouragement for program participation). We also examine spillovers (based on geographic proximity or social network connections) from strongly encouraged to other households. Additional treatments randomly assigned by our research team allow insight into complementarities with interventions providing targeted information and financial incentives for HIV testing.
records.fundingSource: External: United States Agency for International Development via the BASIS research program at University of California, Davis; National Science Foundation; Innovations for Poverty Actions;

records.projectTitle: Interventions to Improve HIV Antiretroviral Therapy Adherence in Beira, Mozambique
records.collaborators: James Riddell (University of Michigan Medical School) Arlete Mahumane (Beira Operational Research Center, National Institute of Health, Mozambique) Hang Yu (Department of Economics, University of Michigan)
records.projectDescription: The long-term goal is to understand the barriers that HIV patients in sub-Saharan Africa face in adhering to antiretroviral therapy (ART) and develop interventions to improve adherence that may be scaled up. Many people living with HIV in sub-Saharan Africa fail to adhere to ART, the medical treatment for HIV that can stop the virus from progressing to AIDS and also prevents transmission to others. This study seeks to identify the barriers to treatment adherence and develop interventions to improve adherence. We recruit patients who are newly diagnosed with HIV infection and prescribed ART from a large health center in Mozambique. For patients who meet the study qualifications, we randomly assign them to receive standard of care versus reminder phone calls, a monetary incentive or both. Other interventions aim to increase knowledge regarding HIV infection and decrease stigma. We track the medication possession ratio (MPR) after six months to evaluate the various interventions. To shed light on the spillovers of the interventions, we offer coupons for the participants to refer their partners to get tested. The experiment result will inform us of behavioral patterns of the population that are economically and physically vulnerable. Effective interventions are ready to be scaled up in HIV-treatment facilities in resource-constrained environments.
records.fundingSource: U-M: MITRE Research Fund, Department of Economics; Institute for Social Research; Global REACH Partnership Development Grant

records.projectTitle: Collaborative Project to Advance the Practice of Hospital Pharmacy
records.collaborators: James Stevenson, U-M
records.projectDescription: This project grew out of an initial collaboration, including funding and assignment of a University of Michigan PharmD graduate student to conduct a baseline assessment in Namibia during June-July 2018. This project will focus on leveraging the findings from the Namibia assessment, as well as a Pakistan assessment in order to advance the diagnostic results into capacity building intervention and funding development for the two countries.
records.fundingSource: UM: William Davidson Institute. External: International Federation of Pharmacists

records.projectTitle: Together Tomorrow
records.collaborators: Lynae Darbes, University of Michigan, Heidi van Rooyen, Human Sciences Research Council, South Africa
records.projectDescription: Together Tomorrow was a cross-sectional survey of men who have sex with men and their partners in South Africa and Namibia. We aimed to identify predictors of HIV sexual risk behavior as well as engagement in HIV treatment and prevention activities (e.g, HIV testing, uptake of antiretrovial therapy, etc.). We recruited couples in KwaZulu-Natal, South Africa and from several sites in Namibia. The context for male same-sex behavior in these countries is very different as South Africa's constitution protects gay rights and gay marriage, whereas Namibia does not (same-sex behavior is technically illegal). To our knowledge this was the first study to examine the relationship context and HIV for male couples in Sub-Saharan Africa.
records.fundingSource: External: This project was funded by DFID in UK, we are aiming to compete for NIH funded for subsequent work.
records.fundingDuration: The initial project has ended, but we are aiming to submit for a longitudinal cohort study through NIH.

records.projectTitle: African Art After Independence, 1957-1977
records.collaborators: Laura De Becker, Kelly Askew, Franc Nunoo-Quarcoo
records.projectDescription: In 1957, Ghana became the first African country to declare independence from its colonizers, spearheading a wave of political uprisings across the continent. By 1970, forty-five of today’s fifty-four African states had regained their independence. The autonomy of these nations coincided with artistic revolutions. Everywhere, artists began rethinking their relationship to the new nation state, to the African continent and to a world fractured by the Cold War. African Art After Independence, 1957-1977 explores how modern African artists were at the forefront of these critical conversations.

records.projectTitle: The US and the War on Terror in Sahel Sahara: Problems and Prospects
records.projectDescription: This is a project sponsored by the Office of Research at UM-Flint. It examines US policy of "War against Terror" in the Sahel including efforts at providing military assistance to countries such as Mali, Niger, Chad and Mauritania. The research seeks to answer certain pertinent questions: Does military intervention necessarily lead to peace, or other unintended consequences such as national/regional violence and insurgency? What are the motivations for the rise of radical Islamist groups such as Boko Haram in Northern Nigeria? What are the social and political implications of US intervention in Sahel Sahara?

records.projectTitle: Writing for Publication Support for African Scholars
records.collaborators: I collaborate the University of Michigan African Presidential Scholars (UMAPS). This has been an 11 year collaboration. The names are too numerous to mention.
records.projectDescription: Many different types of research capacity building programs for African scholars have been established over the years. Although many such programs focus on supporting scholars’ efforts to produce high quality research and to establish their own research priorities, the University of Michigan African Presidential Scholars (UMAPS) program is unique in recognizing the additional need for scholars to develop research writing capacities. Writing is central to being a successful scholar. Yet, unlike research skills, writing for research purposes is seldom explicitly taught. The UMAPS Writing for Publication project consists of a writing workshop for UMAPS scholars and a research agenda with three aims. The first, is to explore ways to support the scholars’ understanding of writing for publication genres and subgenres that support a scholarly career. The second aim is to investigate how scholars can develop a more sensitive appreciation of the rhetorical nature of academic writing, and to see how it is shaped by disciplinary and national cultures. This includes how authors establish their stance and engage with readers to create a convincing text. The third aim is to explore how the scholars’ can extend their knowledge of writing for publication with their own graduate students when they return home. By understanding genres that are functional in a particular disciplinary community, the scholars can be prepared to be writing mentors who are able to explain how to craft a text, thus preparing the next generation of scholars to engage in the scholarly activity of their chosen fields.

records.projectTitle: Ògbóni: Materiality, Dissemblance and Immortality in the Visual Culture of a Nigerian Secret Society
records.projectDescription: In southwestern Nigeria, Ògbóni is an ancient society of honored elders whose mysteries are unknown even to most Yorùbá people who inhabit the region. Ògbóni is neither precisely a religious nor political association, yet its power is said to be greater, and older, than that of kings, colonizers, nations, religions, civilizations, even the gods themselves. For more than a century, scholars have attempted to locate the “secret” of Ògbóni power in figurated brass objects that bespeak mystery and magnificence. Their interpretations seem satisfying, yet don’t fully address Ògbóni claims to be “lords of the Earth” transcending the fray of human history. What is the nature of the power that justifies such claims? I am convinced Ògbóni members—in the dazzling objects they proffer to public view, and in narratives they deploy to explain them—have long misled inquiring scholars and local non-initiates alike. Ògbóni protects itself from incursion via a material lexicon of illegibility, inversion, withdrawal, and dissemblance. Reflexive methodological questions arise in the absence of transparent dialogue: How to look at and describe objects that do not want to be seen or said?

records.projectTitle: Music Time in Africa Archive
records.collaborators: Kelly Askew, University of Michigan
records.projectDescription: The Music Time in Africa Archive consists of digitized audio recordings and scripts from VOA’s oldest continuously operated radio broadcast to Africa. Musicologist Leo Sarkisian created and programmed the weekly 30-minute international radio broadcasts for over forty years. Begun in 1965 and continuing to this day for a full hour, Music Time in Africa intersperses tracks from contemporary African pop recordings with occasional interviews and excerpts from hundreds of hours of original recordings of traditional African music made by Leo Sarkisian in over 34 African countries. With generous support from the National Endowment for the Humanities, almost 700 radio broadcasts (1996-1996) and associated program scripts are now in digital form and available for exploration and listening. An additional 200 full radio programs (lacking printed scripts) are being prepared for eventual release. A newly launched website for the Music Time in Africa Archive provides ample information about the origins and development of the MTIA Archive. The website also provides direct access to a custom built online platform (click on “Listen”) that supports faceted metadata browsing, full-text search and display of the program scripts, and streaming audio access to the radio programs.
records.fundingSource: External: National Endowment for the Humanities

records.projectTitle: Creating a Network for Gender-Based Violence Prevention at Universities across Sub-Saharan Africa
records.collaborators: Sarah Compton - UM Department of OB/GYN; Eugene Darteh - University of Cape Coast, Ghana; Ruth Owusu-Antwi - Kwame Nkrumah University of Science and Technology, Ghana; Sibongile Kamusoko - National University of Science and Technology, Zimbabwe; Sinegugu Duma - University of KwaZulu-Natal, South Africa; Akanni Akinyemi - Obafemi Awolowo University, Nigeria
records.projectDescription: Gender-based violence (GBV), including sexual violence, is a serious problem affecting the safety, dignity, health, and well-being of millions of individuals around the globe, with high rates among university students. Rates of GBV in sub-Saharan Africa are higher than in other regions; South Africa has the highest rate of GBV in the world. There is a recognized need for programs that explicitly address GBV among university students in sub-Saharan Africa. However, currently universities are working independently to address this problem. There is a need for a systematic evaluation and a network to share best practices, lessons learned, and resources. This proposal brings together five universities from western (Ghana, Nigeria), eastern (Zimbabwe), and southern (South Africa) parts of sub-Saharan Africa. The team has research, educational, and administrative experience related to GBV programming on university campuses. Thus, the overall objective of this proposal is to create a GBV research and prevention network to conduct initial research on GBV knowledge, attitudes, and behaviors among university students in sub-Saharan Africa. This will help establish a formal Sub-Saharan University GBV Research and Prevention Network to share needs, resources, and best practices to improve the research environment and strengthen individual and institutional research capabilities across network partners as well as expand to other interested institutions.
records.fundingSource: U-M: pending ASC (ASRI seed grant). External: pending NIH application for Sexual Violence Research Initiative

records.projectTitle: Early life Aflatoxin B Exposure
records.collaborators: Oluwakemi Rotimi, Covenant University, Nigeria
records.projectDescription: The Incidence of liver cancer is on the rise in sub-Saharan Africa, where it is the fourth most frequent cancer. The increase in incidence has been associated with exposures to both hepatitis B virus and aflatoxin B1 (AFB1). Aflatoxins are products of certain types of molds belonging to the Aspergillus species that contaminate food and feed stuff in tropical countries particularly in Africa and southeast Asia. This project seeks to understand the effect of perinatal aflatoxin (AFB1) exposure (gestation through weaning) on DNA methylation of hepatic tumor suppressor genes in adulthood using an animal model. The central hypothesis is that perinatal exposure to AFB1 will alter the rat liver epigenome and increase susceptibility to the development of liver cancer later in life. The specific hypothesis for this pilot project is that perinatal exposure to AFB1 will alter the methylation of three tumor suppressor genes, quantified immediately post-exposure (at 3 weeks of age) and in early adulthood (at 3 months of age).
records.fundingSource: U-M: ASC Seed Grant; NIH K43 Grant (PI: Rotimi)

records.projectTitle: Grassroots Perspective of Central African Theatre and Performing Arts: Multivocality and Social-Political Dynamics
records.collaborators: Joseph Ndundu Kivwila, Institut National des Arts/Kinshasa- D.R. Congo
records.projectDescription: The project originates primarily from my experiences and observations as a theatre director. It does not deal with the political or the democratization process. It rather deals with the contribution of particular artists, writers, and performers to expressing the unspoken feelings of the common people, as well as their critiques of the institutions and individual leaders. After having worked to support the same leaders in their productions, they turned against them, not openly like opposing political leaders do. They turn to the art of the traditional discourse full of innuendoes, rhetorical tropes, as well as the power of rumor, to create works that apparently maintained the same line of social depiction but that were perceived as very critical to the political establishments. This is something that Africanist and drama scholarships have missed in their study of political and social processes of the last three decades as well as the creative works of African dramatists. Performances are of meaningful interest for they remove the political power and process from the official and state institutional dramaturgy to put them on the artistic stage so as to depict and critique their weaknesses in addition to reminding their obligations in front of the common people.

records.projectTitle: The Health and Poverty Effects of a Large-scale Cookstove Initiative in Rwanda
records.collaborators: Sudhanshu (Ashu) Handa: UNC-Chapel Hill, Leena Nylander-French: UNC-Chapel Hill, Karin Yeatts: UNC-Chapel Hill, Joe Pedit: UNC-Chapel Hill
records.projectDescription: Approximately half the world's population uses solid fuels (wood, animal dung, coal, biomass) as their principal fuel for lighting, cooking, and heating. Dependence on these fuels and traditional cooking technologies is responsible for a host of health issues, including acute respiratory infection among children under five, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease among adults, low birth weight, and other negative health outcomes. As a result, household air pollution and ambient particulate matter are two of the most important contributors to the global disease burden. Improved cooking technology, if used correctly and taken to scale, has the potential for significantly reducing the global disease burden. This project will evaluate the impact of a private sector cookstove and fuel distribution intervention on exposure to airborne pollutants, health and poverty. Inyenyeri, a private social entrepreneurship venture, implements a fuel-side cookstove venture in Western Rwanda using the Mimi Moto stove. This study will assess the exposure to particulate matter, health and poverty among households who adopt the Mimi Moto stove.

records.projectTitle: Green Space, Trauma and Mental Health in Kigali City, Rwanda: A Cross-Sectional Comparative Study with Implications for Detroit
records.collaborators: Samuel Habimana, University of Rwanda, School of Public Health, Department of Community Health, Kigali-Rwanda; Salman Qureshi, Department of Geography, Humboldt University Berlin; Juliette Roddy, Northern Arizona University; Emmanuel Biracyaza, Eugene Rutembesa; Department of Clinical Psychology, School of Medicine and Pharmacy, College of Medicine and Health Sciences, University of Rwanda, Kigali-Rwanda
records.projectDescription: Detroit, Michigan, and Kigali, Rwanda, are distant cities that share a legacy of historical trauma and issues of intergenerational poverty, as well as challenges posed by accelerating redevelopment. This project supports the implementation of a multi-method study of green space and mental health carried out by community partners in Kigali, while simultaneously developing a comparative framework for application to Detroit and other post-industrial cities. In particular, we seek to understand how deliberate investment in restorative green spaces in Kigali, such as the newly created Garden of Memory, may yield benefits for the mental health and resilience of residents burdened by trauma. Urban green space (UGS) of Kigali City will first be mapped; residents will be recruited to participate in a survey measuring uses of urban green space; and subsequent quantitative analysis will focus on barriers to green space, expressed benefits of green space in terms of mental health restoration, and the relationship between green space and community resilience. Such findings have significant implications for a city like Detroit as it faces redevelopment pressures of its own.
records.fundingSource: U-M: Pending funding from African Students Center and Poverty Solutions at UM-AA.

records.projectTitle: The US and the War on Terror in Sahel Sahara: Problems and Prospects
records.projectDescription: This is a project sponsored by the Office of Research at UM-Flint. It examines US policy of "War against Terror" in the Sahel including efforts at providing military assistance to countries such as Mali, Niger, Chad and Mauritania. The research seeks to answer certain pertinent questions: Does military intervention necessarily lead to peace, or other unintended consequences such as national/regional violence and insurgency? What are the motivations for the rise of radical Islamist groups such as Boko Haram in Northern Nigeria? What are the social and political implications of US intervention in Sahel Sahara?

records.projectTitle: African Art After Independence, 1957-1977
records.collaborators: Laura De Becker, Kelly Askew, Franc Nunoo-Quarcoo
records.projectDescription: In 1957, Ghana became the first African country to declare independence from its colonizers, spearheading a wave of political uprisings across the continent. By 1970, forty-five of today’s fifty-four African states had regained their independence. The autonomy of these nations coincided with artistic revolutions. Everywhere, artists began rethinking their relationship to the new nation state, to the African continent and to a world fractured by the Cold War. African Art After Independence, 1957-1977 explores how modern African artists were at the forefront of these critical conversations.

records.projectTitle: Ecological Determinates and Arboreal Feeding Positional Behaviors in Pan troglodytes
records.collaborators: Lauren Sarringhaus, University of Michigan; Sharifah Namaganda, Makerere University
records.projectDescription: Living hominoids (apes and humans) are distinguished by an upright torso and versatile locomotion. Observations of living apes have led to the hypothesis that such versatility evolved to feed on ripe fruit in the forest canopy. However, recent fossil discoveries show that the earliest apes to evolve an upright torso fed on leaves and fruit in a partially open, seasonal environment. To determine how locomotor versatility relates to foraging behavior, this study will investigate positional versatility and foraging success in wild chimpanzees, the living ape with the broadest habitat preferences. The goals are to (a) collect data on versatile arboreal positional behavior from chimpanzees in forest (Ngogo, Kibale, Uganda) and woodland (Fongoli, Senegal) habitats, and (b) characterize the ecological context of these behaviors in order to address two sets of questions: (1) How does variation in arboreal structural complexity (e.g. variation in canopy height), food type (fruit vs. leaves) and canopy location, correlate with positional behavior in woodland and forest chimpanzees? (2) Are the joint excursions hypothesized to occur during versatile behaviors executed as predicted based on anatomical inference? Studying chimpanzee movement in multiple contexts and habitats will assist in predicting why hominoids evolved innovative positional behaviors and may increase our understanding of why early hominins transitioned to a less arboreal existence.

records.projectTitle: Heritage’s History in Colonial West Africa: The Archives of A/ONTOM
records.projectDescription: The “Association nationale pour le développement du tourisme outre-mer” (ANTOM) was formed in 1955 to encourage international tourism to the French colonies, including those in West Africa; in 1957, it was renamed ONTOM, “Office national de tourisme outre-mer”. Even though A/ONTOM only existed for five years, its records and activities are important sources for historicizing the notion of “patrimoine” as it was understood in a critical historical period. This ongoing archival research on the A/ONTOM archives and related personal archives focuses on the careers, personal trajectories, and larger social projects of colonial officials and other professionals working for and with A/ONTOM. A focus on the work of both French and African professionals in the field of tourism and heritage preservation during the period leading up to and right after Independence will broaden our understanding of colonial tourism (hitherto understood as focused primarily on wildlife and theorized in terms of empire-building and nationalism) and historicize later attempts at constituting selected socio-cultural practices, discourses, spaces, and material objects as “heritage”.

records.projectTitle: Uthando Lwethu: a Couples-Based Intervention Aiming to Increase Uptake of Couples-Based HIV Testing and Counseling (CHTC)
records.collaborators: Heidi van Rooyen, Human Sciences Research Council, South Africa, Mallory Johnson, University of California, San Francisco
records.projectDescription: Uthando Lwethu (“Our love” in Zulu) was a successful NIH-funded clinical trail that successfully increased the uptake of couples-based HIV testing and counseling (CHTC) among a sample (N=332 couples) of heterosexual couples in rural KwaZulu-Natal, South Africa. Rates of HIV testing in this area were low even in the context of a high prevalence HIV epidemic. Disclosure of serostatus between couples is often low due to high rates of HIV-related stigma.The couples-focused intervention focused on improving communication skills and positive relationship dynamics via group-based and couples counseling. Retention at 9-month follow-up was over 90%, even among a heterogeneous sample (e.g., only 10% of couples were married and only 20% lived together). The counseling was delivered by lay counselors, increasing feasibility and sustainability of the intervention. Future plans include conducting an implementation science study to ensure that the intervention can be successfully delivered in the community—outside of the controlled research context (funding not yet obtained).
records.fundingSource: External: NIH/National Institute of Mental Health

records.collaborators: I have multiple projects with numerous collaborators, none at UM although I have discussed potential projects with John Kingston (Anthropology), Laura MacLatchy (Anthropology), and Brian Stewart (Archaeology)
records.projectDescription: I work to understand how landscapes and ecosystems in Africa have changed over the past 10 million years, particularly in response to climate change and often associated with understanding the the context for human evolution over the past 10 million years. My work also involves establishing the age and environmental conditions of human ancestors. records.projectTitle: 5000 Years of African Literature
records.projectDescription: The goal is to see whether it makes sense to treat the continuous evidence of writing and, soon after, literature in Africa from the Egyptians on as a coherent tradition. Hence, early evidence comes from Egyptian, then Semitic scripts from Ethiopia, and Classical Greek and Latin in North Africa. This is followed by Arabic (both sides of the Sahara), early efforts in the vernacular, and, more recently, European languages. Obviously, one question here is whether it makes sense to connect different languages in the same region--as it does, for instance, in the Ancient Near East or in Europe from the 12th century on. Another, unique to Africa, is whether the Sahara provides an insuperable cultural barrier. The account will end with a look at the diaspora, widely studied for the 20th century, less so for the 19th.

records.projectTitle: Book Projects on Aesthetics, Politics and Culture in Africa
records.projectDescription: My most recent book under submission: Visual art has a ubiquitous political cast today. But which politics? While political values are assumed, indeed trumpeted to the point where they are meant to follow automatically (if not magically) from the contemporary visual art object, the many and various things meant by politics are seldom unpacked and evaluated. Drawing on a wide berth of examples, from the NEA 4 and the question of offense-as-dissent, to M.F. Husain and the Hindu nationalist Indian right wing, from humanitarian art to peri-urban photography posed in relation to radical politics, from the work of William Kentridge (drenched in war, violence and race) to the artworld immolations of Bansky--this book seeks case by case clarity on what is meant by politics, and how to evaluate its presumption or aspiration. The book therefore raises Hegelian questions about the capacity of the visual medium to "speak" politically or acquire political agency while respecting the importance of the political voice. What is new in this book also derives from its genealogical component, which seeks to understand art practices today in the light of two opposing inheritances: that of the avant-gardes and their politicization of the experimental art object, and of Eighteenth century aesthetics, which frames aesthetic experience as radically autonomous from the rest of life (therefore apolitical), and celebrates this encomium of aesthetic experience as an icon of free choice, therefore of individual liberty. I show how this pair of legacies is crucial to understanding the tensions in voice and character of contemporary art.
records.fundingSource: U-M: research funds, grant from African Studies Center to support documentary film training I do with township students

records.projectTitle: Election Related Violence and Security in Africa
records.collaborators: Grant Masterson-Electoral Institute for Sustainable Democracy in Africa; Rod Alence, University of the Witwatersrand, Johannesburg, South Africa
records.projectDescription: Our project leverages election observer missions (EOMs) to assess the degree and kinds of election related violence that occur and the levels of security that are available during national and local elections in Africa. It collates results from roughly 300 polling stations in countries where the Electoral Institute for Sustainable Democracy in Africa (EISA) sends EOMS. Election observers on the ground gather information about security arrangements, incidents of political violence, and public infrastructure and services in the areas served by individual polling stations. The information they collect is geocoded and linked to local election returns. The resulting data sets allow academics and practitioners to analyze the relationship between elections and violence and to identify local factors – such as public infrastructure, security and services – that can inform efforts to promote sustainable peace in Africa.
records.fundingSource: External: Carnegie Corporation of New York

records.projectTitle: From Africa to Patagonia: Voices of Displacement
records.collaborators: Paulina Alberto, U-M; Lorenzo Garcia-Amaya, U-M; Nicholas Henriksen, U-M; Katherine Jenckes, U-M; Victoria Langland, U-M; Ryan Szpiech, U-M; Brady West, U-M; Daan Wissing, North-West University, South Africa
records.projectDescription: This project documents the linguistic and cultural practices of a 120-year old Afrikaans expatriate community in Patagonia, Argentina. The Afrikaans spoken by the community gives a unique window on the late 19th century form of Afrikaans (before the influence of English and standardization in South Africa in the 20th century). The community's cultural identity practices also provide information about Afrikaner identity formation before the advent of Apartheid in the 20th century in South Africa. Funded through U-M Humanities Collaboratory, and involving 3 post-docs, 5 PhD students, and around 30 undergraduate students.
records.fundingDuration: Funding will end at the end of Winter 2020 semester.

records.projectTitle: Language and Identity in a Post-Apartheid City
records.collaborators: Ian Bekker, North-West University, South Africa
records.projectDescription: This project aims to document shifting linguistic practices in Potchefstroom, a regional city in South Africa. Ever since the end of Apartheid in 1994, South African society has been undergoing rapid change and reorganization. In this project, we track how these changes are reflected in the language usage patterns in Potchefstroom, and also how the relationship between language and social/political/cultural identity is changing. How do language users use their varied linguistic repertoires to navigate the shifting and complex social landscapes in a post-Apartheid society?

records.projectTitle: Nasalization in Two Socio-Ethnic Varieties of Afrikaans
records.collaborators: Patrice Beddor, U-M; Daan Wissing, North-West University, South Africa; Ian Bekker, North-West University, South Africa
records.projectDescription: This project investigates the production and perception of anticipatory nasalization in two socio-ethnic varieties of Afrikaans, so called "White Afrikaans" and so-called "Kleurling Afrikaans". Using pneumotachography, we show that Kleurling Afrikaans is characterized by early and more extensive nasal airflow. We then use eye-tracking technology to show that Afrikaans listeners rely on nasal coarticulation perceptually. We're also exploring whether listeners can use nasal coarticulatory information differentially based on the identity of the speaker -- i.e. rely more it when listening to a speaker of White than Kleurling Afrikaans. This research sheds light on the social structure of phonetic variation, and on how socially structured variation can mediate the link between speech perception and production.

Utilizing the principle of consciousness raising—that women themselves must understand their own oppression and, more importantly, how they themselves participate in this oppression—the GCP engages high school females in conceptualizing how gender, with other identities, impact various facets of their lives. records.projectTitle: Writing for Publication Support for African Scholars
records.collaborators: I collaborate the University of Michigan African Presidential Scholars (UMAPS). This has been an 11 year collaboration. The names are too numerous to mention.
records.projectDescription: Many different types of research capacity building programs for African scholars have been established over the years. Although many such programs focus on supporting scholars’ efforts to produce high quality research and to establish their own research priorities, the University of Michigan African Presidential Scholars (UMAPS) program is unique in recognizing the additional need for scholars to develop research writing capacities. Writing is central to being a successful scholar. Yet, unlike research skills, writing for research purposes is seldom explicitly taught. The UMAPS Writing for Publication project consists of a writing workshop for UMAPS scholars and a research agenda with three aims. The first, is to explore ways to support the scholars’ understanding of writing for publication genres and subgenres that support a scholarly career. The second aim is to investigate how scholars can develop a more sensitive appreciation of the rhetorical nature of academic writing, and to see how it is shaped by disciplinary and national cultures. This includes how authors establish their stance and engage with readers to create a convincing text. The third aim is to explore how the scholars’ can extend their knowledge of writing for publication with their own graduate students when they return home. By understanding genres that are functional in a particular disciplinary community, the scholars can be prepared to be writing mentors who are able to explain how to craft a text, thus preparing the next generation of scholars to engage in the scholarly activity of their chosen fields.

records.projectTitle: Creating a Network for Gender-Based Violence Prevention at Universities across Sub-Saharan Africa
records.collaborators: Sarah Compton - UM Department of OB/GYN; Eugene Darteh - University of Cape Coast, Ghana; Ruth Owusu-Antwi - Kwame Nkrumah University of Science and Technology, Ghana; Sibongile Kamusoko - National University of Science and Technology, Zimbabwe; Sinegugu Duma - University of KwaZulu-Natal, South Africa; Akanni Akinyemi - Obafemi Awolowo University, Nigeria
records.projectDescription: Gender-based violence (GBV), including sexual violence, is a serious problem affecting the safety, dignity, health, and well-being of millions of individuals around the globe, with high rates among university students. Rates of GBV in sub-Saharan Africa are higher than in other regions; South Africa has the highest rate of GBV in the world. There is a recognized need for programs that explicitly address GBV among university students in sub-Saharan Africa. However, currently universities are working independently to address this problem. There is a need for a systematic evaluation and a network to share best practices, lessons learned, and resources. This proposal brings together five universities from western (Ghana, Nigeria), eastern (Zimbabwe), and southern (South Africa) parts of sub-Saharan Africa. The team has research, educational, and administrative experience related to GBV programming on university campuses. Thus, the overall objective of this proposal is to create a GBV research and prevention network to conduct initial research on GBV knowledge, attitudes, and behaviors among university students in sub-Saharan Africa. This will help establish a formal Sub-Saharan University GBV Research and Prevention Network to share needs, resources, and best practices to improve the research environment and strengthen individual and institutional research capabilities across network partners as well as expand to other interested institutions.
records.fundingSource: U-M: pending ASC (ASRI seed grant). External: pending NIH application for Sexual Violence Research Initiative

records.projectDescription: Together Tomorrow was a cross-sectional survey of men who have sex with men and their partners in South Africa and Namibia. We aimed to identify predictors of HIV sexual risk behavior as well as engagement in HIV treatment and prevention activities (e.g, HIV testing, uptake of antiretrovial therapy, etc.). We recruited couples in KwaZulu-Natal, South Africa and from several sites in Namibia. The context for male same-sex behavior in these countries is very different as South Africa's constitution protects gay rights and gay marriage, whereas Namibia does not (same-sex behavior is technically illegal). To our knowledge this was the first study to examine the relationship context and HIV for male couples in Sub-Saharan Africa.

records.projectTitle: Cumulative Socioeconomic Exposures, Cash Transfer Interventions, and Later-Life Cognitive Decline and Dementia Risk in a Low-Income Region of South Africa
records.collaborators: Dr. Molly Rosenberg (Indiana University); Dr. Lisa Berkman, Dr. David Canning, Dr. Meagan Farrell (Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health); Dr. Audrey Pettifor (UNC Gillings School of Global Public Health); Dr. Chodziwadziwa Kabudula, Dr. Stephen Tollman, Dr. Kathleen Kahn (University of the Witswatersrand)
records.projectDescription: Our objective is to identify how SES affects ADRD risk, using longitudinal exposures (household assets, food security, and employment) and randomized and quasi-randomized cash transfer exposures accumulated over time in mid-to-later-life in rural South Africa. Our central hypothesis is that greater cumulative exposure to advantageous socioeconomic conditions will protect against ADRD, with differential effects for men and women for household-level SES resources that may not be equally allocated between genders. To test this central hypothesis, we will leverage a unique opportunity to create one of the first longitudinal data platforms to study ADRD risk in Africa: we will link high-quality memory and ADRD outcome data collected from 2015-21 in the population-representative “Health and Aging in Africa: A Longitudinal Study of an INDEPTH Community in South Africa” (HAALSI; N=5059) to detailed SES exposure data collected since 2000 in the “Agincourt Health and Sociodemographic Surveillance System” (AHDSS; a census with 100% population coverage) and the HPTN 068 randomized controlled trial of cash transfers to households from 2011-15 (N=863 households in both HPTN 068 and HAALSI). These three datasets can be linked because the AHDSS census is the sampling frame for HAALSI and HPTN 068; we have confirmed linkage feasibility in our preliminary data. Specifically, we aim to: 1) Determine the roles of mid-to-later life cumulative household assets, employment, and food security (2000-13; AHDSS) in memory decline and ADRD risk (2015-21; HAALSI); 2) Determine whether exposure to cash transfer interventions affect memory decline and ADRD by leveraging 2a) randomized cash transfers in HPTN 068; 2b) expansion of the South African Old Age Pension for men; 2c) expansion of the South African Child Support Grant for mothers; and 3) Determine how gender modifies the effects of household-level SES exposures in Aims 1 & 2a.

records.projectTitle: African Art After Independence, 1957-1977
records.collaborators: Laura De Becker, Kelly Askew, Franc Nunoo-Quarcoo
records.projectDescription: In 1957, Ghana became the first African country to declare independence from its colonizers, spearheading a wave of political uprisings across the continent. By 1970, forty-five of today’s fifty-four African states had regained their independence. The autonomy of these nations coincided with artistic revolutions. Everywhere, artists began rethinking their relationship to the new nation state, to the African continent and to a world fractured by the Cold War. African Art After Independence, 1957-1977 explores how modern African artists were at the forefront of these critical conversations.

records.projectTitle: Transformations in Poverty and Property Rights in Rural Tanzania
records.collaborators: Kelly Askew, University of Michigan; Rie Odgaard Danish Institute of International Studies, Faustin Maganga, St. Johns University, Tanzania
records.projectDescription: Since 2009, I have been working with an interdisciplinary team of researchers to analyze the impact of property right formalization on poverty in rural Tanzania. From 2010 through 2015, we undertook more than 2000 household surveys in 40 villages covering eight districts in four regions of the country. We also generated hundreds of semi-structured interviews with village leaders, government officials, bankers, NGOS, and donors. Between 2016 and 2019, we returned to interview a selection of our original households in 18 of the original villages in the regions of Manyara, Dodoma and Mbeya to better assess the impact of formalization roughly five years after our first interviews. This longitudinal approach has allowed us to also assess the shifting socioeconomic conditions in the country and to enter into the discussions on the degree of poverty and inequality. We have started a new wave of interviews in 2019 in a fifth region, Iringa.

records.projectTitle: Ethnography in Indigenous Shrines in Togo
records.collaborators: Eric J. Montgomery (Michigan State University)
records.projectDescription: This research sought to ascertain the cultural meanings surrounding the gods, practices, and metaphysics of vodu as they relate to daily life in an ethnic Ewe fishing community on the coast of southern Togo. Specifically, we focus on a relatively modern denomination of vodu, Gorovodu (lit: “kola nut vodu”), a spirit possession and medicinal order found among many cultural groups along the Bight of Benin and in the Volta region of contemporary Ghana and Togo. Gorovodu is a faith, moral code, and vibrant philosophy for living. It is also an inclusive system of law and an atikevodu, or medicinal vodu order (lit: “tree root vodu”). Originating as several distinct witch-finding cults in the savanna regions of north Ghana, Gorovodu emerged and coalesced from an enduring history of economic, political, and social relations between coastal groups and those of the interior. The Gorovodu gods spread from the cultural groups of the northern savanna to those of the southern forest belt and coasts throughout the twentieth century through the establishment of shrines built to house and venerate them. As the gods—and the ritual beliefs and practices that surrounded them—embraced local cultural contexts and religious systems, they took on new forms, narratives, and meanings.

records.projectTitle: Jewish Literature in the Global South
records.projectDescription: Following the Afro-Diasporic tradition of “each one teach one”, the project “Jewish Literature in the Global South” is meant to engage with and raise public awareness of Jewish literature and culture from Africa, Iraq, and India. The final product will be an iteration of a website that includes archival materials; collaborative translations incorporated into the curriculum; and visually rich essays produced by scholars and students The literature examined will be original works written by African Jewish communities. Through the tradition of public readings of novels and recorded oral folklore to men and women unable to read the written word, Judeo-Arabic culture’s tradition of “each one teach one” has embraced equitable access to knowledge for non-readers. As such, African history provides positive lessons to teach the field of Humanities through the incorporation of a tradition of inclusionary teaching and learning practices. Included within the archival collection will be rare testimonies from the experiences of Jews living through the Holocaust in North Africa; philosophical treatises on Judaism’s mission to end racism; folk stories like the 1001 Nights; and histories of Jewish communities throughout the world.

records.projectDescription: I'm writing a book about the history of Idi Amin's Uganda; I am also engaged in an ongoing project to digitise and display photographs concerning the Amin regime in the Uganda Museum. The latter exhibition project is titled 'The Unseen Archive of Idi Amin'; it has thus far involved three exhibitions in Uganda's museums, with a fourth instalment in June this year.

records.projectTitle: Digitisation of Local Government Archives in Western Uganda
records.collaborators: Evarist Ngabirano and Moses Akugizibwe, Mountains of the Moon University
records.projectDescription: From 2009 forward , African Heritiage and Humanities Initiave of the ASC has funded and supported an ongoing project to digitse the endangered archives of local government in western Uganda. Over the course of more than a decade of work—and with substantial support from the Center for Research Libaries—a team at Mountains of the Moon University has digitised over 500,000 pages. It is—remarkably—the largest digital repository of government documents in Africa.

records.projectTitle: Digitization of the Wewspaper Archives of Makerere University Library
records.collaborators: Monica Naluwooza, Head of Special Collections, Makerere University Library
records.projectDescription: Since the 1930s, Makerere University has been collecting newspapers published in Uganda—it is the copyright library for Uganda. The newspaper archives it has accumulated over the course of time are of huge importance: they are a record of African intellectual and political life, created and edited by African journalists. Because the newspapers were printed on high-acid paper, they are in are poor condition. We intend to launch a project next year to digitise this vitally important archival collection, and are currently looking for financial support.
records.fundingSource: Eernal: Funding pending. Professor Peterson will support part of the work from his MacArthur award.

records.projectTitle: Ecological Determinates and Arboreal Feeding Positional Behaviors in Pan troglodytes
records.collaborators: Lauren Sarringhaus, University of Michigan; Sharifah Namaganda, Makerere University
records.projectDescription: Living hominoids (apes and humans) are distinguished by an upright torso and versatile locomotion. Observations of living apes have led to the hypothesis that such versatility evolved to feed on ripe fruit in the forest canopy. However, recent fossil discoveries show that the earliest apes to evolve an upright torso fed on leaves and fruit in a partially open, seasonal environment. To determine how locomotor versatility relates to foraging behavior, this study will investigate positional versatility and foraging success in wild chimpanzees, the living ape with the broadest habitat preferences. The goals are to (a) collect data on versatile arboreal positional behavior from chimpanzees in forest (Ngogo, Kibale, Uganda) and woodland (Fongoli, Senegal) habitats, and (b) characterize the ecological context of these behaviors in order to address two sets of questions: (1) How does variation in arboreal structural complexity (e.g. variation in canopy height), food type (fruit vs. leaves) and canopy location, correlate with positional behavior in woodland and forest chimpanzees? (2) Are the joint excursions hypothesized to occur during versatile behaviors executed as predicted based on anatomical inference? Studying chimpanzee movement in multiple contexts and habitats will assist in predicting why hominoids evolved innovative positional behaviors and may increase our understanding of why early hominins transitioned to a less arboreal existence.

records.projectTitle: Elementary Swahili Textbook
records.collaborators: Through funding, I have had students research assistants
records.projectDescription: The major hurdle in teaching Swahili has been finding textbooks that are both appealing and comprehensive. For this reason I have been developing material for my own textbook for the teaching of Elementary Swahili, as the most commonly used textbooks are now several decades old. This new textbook would be coupled with classroom readers featuring accompanying audio and visual materials for Elementary level.

records.projectTitle: Organizing and Cataloguing the Archives of Uganda's Judiciary
records.collaborators: Sauda Nabukenya, PhD student in History, University of Michigan; Pius Bigirimana, Permanent Secretary, Judiciary of Uganda
records.projectDescription: The University of Michigan is currently engaged in an ongoing project to organise, catalogue, and make accessible the endangered archives of Uganda's Judiciary. Over the course of four years, a team has worked with two archives: the records of Uganda's High Court, which run from 1940 forward to 1989; and the archives of the Mengo Court, which run from 1910 to 1967. The High Court archive project was completed 2019, when a collection of 70,000 case files was deposited in the National Archives of Uganda. We are engaged in ongoing work with the Mengo Court, a collection of 50,000 files that records the deliberation of Africa's oldest customary legal court. We hope to begin digitising this collection later in 2020.
records.fundingSource: External: Judiciary of Uganda; John D. and Catherine T. MacArthur fellowship

records.projectTitle: Professional Development in Africa

records.projectDescription: This project will be working with 10 early career scholars from all over Africa in developmental science. It is focused on research training.

records.projectTitle: Research on East African Catarrhine and Hominoid Evolution
records.collaborators: The REACHE project has generated significant collaborations and intellectual infrastructure, involving multiple research teams including Kieran McNulty, David Fox and Martha Tappan (University of Minnesota); Isaiah Nengo (De Anza College); Ellen Miller (Wake Forest University); Thomas Lehmann (Senckenberg-Forschungsinstitut und Naturmuseum); Nancy Stevens (Miami University Ohio); Emma Mbua and Frederick Manthi (National Museums of Kenya); Robert Kityo and Amon Mugume (Makerere University); James Rossie (SUNY Stony Brook); Mercedes Gutierrez (Tufts University); Susanne Cote (University of Calgary); Daniel Peppe and Steven Driese (Baylor University); Alisa WInkler and Bonnie Jacobs (Southern Methodist University); Alan Deino (Berkeley Geochronology Center); Pierre-Olivier Antoine (Universite Montpellier); Maeva Orliac (Royal Museum for Central Africa); John Kingston and William Sanders (University of Michigan).
records.projectDescription: The goal of this project is to create a multi-disciplinary, multi-national collaboration to fully develop a regional geochronology, paleoecology, paleoenvironment, and biogeography of early Miocene catarrhine and hominoid evolution in East Africa. Over 6 six years, consistent paleoenvironmental and geologic data was collected at multiple early Miocene sites in Kenya and Uganda in order to develop a more detailed model of how the environment may have influenced the early evolution of the oldest apes. Major findings include the realization that the early Miocene was dominate by much higher seasonality than previously recognized, with early apes evolving their signature adaptations in woodland rather than forested environments.

records.projectTitle: Scaling Solar Energy for Health-care Needs in Disadvantaged Communities
records.collaborators: Emmanuel Miyingo, Faculty of Engineering and Design, Makerere Universiity, Kampala, Uganda; Prof. Louise Ackers, University of Salford, UK; Dr. Joel Bazira, Mbarara University of Science and Technology , Mbarara, Uganda; Ronald Owachglu, Lugogo Vocational Training Institute, Kampala, Uganda; Onesmus Owokunda, International Hospital of Kampala, Uganda; Dr. Joseph Kolars, UM Medical School.
records.projectDescription: The aim of this work is to research how to scale the supply of sustainable solar-generated electricity to meet critical needs in rural–off-grid health care clinics. The PI and his students are collaborating with students and faculty in Uganda for the past 10 years. The effort involves both curriculum development and research. Recent Progress: The PI formed a working in-country team consisting of Emmanuel, Miyingo, Makerere University, Owachglu Ronald, of the Lugogo Technical College, Kampala, Yale Global Scholar Mugabwa Nelson, a business-studies student at Kyambojo University in Kampala, and Owokunda Onesmus a clinical trainee at International Hospital of Kampala. Our team carried out field work at two locations in western Uganda, namely at the Kagote and Kataraka government health-care clinics in the Kabarole District near Fort Portal, Uganda. These collaborations have provided valuable data on a wide range of clinical functions requiring reliable electricity supplies, each scaled to a particular need. Examples, include: LED lighting and security, solar-powered baby warmers, efficient thermoelectric refrigeration systems, and power for pathology lab equipment. The different applications constitute the implementation of an affordable, sustainable public-health facility microgrid. It is a first for Uganda, a model that is well matched to community clinic needs where there is essentially no reliable conventional grid-based electrical supply.
records.fundingSource: U-M: African Studies Center. External: Pending from NSF, USAID (Power Africa) and various charities/foundations in the UK and EU.

records.projectTitle: The Asian Presence in East Africa: History, Identity and Community Fifty Years after the Expulsion
records.collaborators: James Ocita, Makerere University
records.projectDescription: In August 2022, Uganda will mark the 50th anniversary of the 1972 expulsion of Asians by decree of then President Idi Amin. While several Ugandan Asians returned to Uganda in the late eighties and nineties, many settled permanently in the lands of their exile. Our project considers the Asian experience in East Africa -- its rich history, literature and cultural production in all its diversity. We gather together an interdisciplinary group of scholars to speak to this experience and will publish a Special Issue of the Journal of the African Literature Association on the topic to be published in 2022. We also hope to launch the Special Issue and to mark the anniversary at an event or symposium at Makerere in August 2022.
records.fundingDuration: Funding anticipated to end late 2022 (after the planned event).

records.projectTitle: Uganda Broadcasting Corporation Archive Project
records.collaborators: Dr. Richard Vokes, University of Western Australia; Dr. Edgar Taylor, Makerere University; Tom Bray, University of Michigan; Nelson Abiti, Uganda Museum; Winston Agaba, Managing Director, Uganda Broadcasting Corporation
records.projectDescription: The UBC holds a massive archive of photographic, sound and film material in analogue formats. Much of this material is decaying badly. In 2018 U-M launched a project to digitise the very substantial collection of still film negatives held by UBC; to date 25,000 photographic stills have been scanned, and work is ongoing. In the next steps of the project we hope to attract funding to digitise 100 film reels from the 1970s. These materials are currently in the custody of Tom Bray at the Duderstat Center. I am currently in dialogue with Spring Film, a UK based film production house, in hopes that they will produce a documentary film drawn from this material (which will in turn result in the digitisation of the old reels).
records.fundingSource: External: MacArthur award to Derek Peterson

records.projectTitle: Writing for Publication Support for African Scholars
records.collaborators: I collaborate the University of Michigan African Presidential Scholars (UMAPS). This has been an 11 year collaboration. The names are too numerous to mention.
records.projectDescription: Many different types of research capacity building programs for African scholars have been established over the years. Although many such programs focus on supporting scholars’ efforts to produce high quality research and to establish their own research priorities, the University of Michigan African Presidential Scholars (UMAPS) program is unique in recognizing the additional need for scholars to develop research writing capacities. Writing is central to being a successful scholar. Yet, unlike research skills, writing for research purposes is seldom explicitly taught. The UMAPS Writing for Publication project consists of a writing workshop for UMAPS scholars and a research agenda with three aims. The first, is to explore ways to support the scholars’ understanding of writing for publication genres and subgenres that support a scholarly career. The second aim is to investigate how scholars can develop a more sensitive appreciation of the rhetorical nature of academic writing, and to see how it is shaped by disciplinary and national cultures. This includes how authors establish their stance and engage with readers to create a convincing text. The third aim is to explore how the scholars’ can extend their knowledge of writing for publication with their own graduate students when they return home. By understanding genres that are functional in a particular disciplinary community, the scholars can be prepared to be writing mentors who are able to explain how to craft a text, thus preparing the next generation of scholars to engage in the scholarly activity of their chosen fields.

records.projectTitle: Exploring U5 Deaths in the Slums of Kampala Using Social Autopsy
records.collaborators: Amy Young, Fogarty Trainee; Peter Waiswa, Makerere University
records.projectDescription: This is a study to identify all deaths of children under age 5 in two slum neighborhoods of Kampala that are within a short distance of the country's largest hospital, attempting to understand why children living within close proximity of healthcare are still dying. The methods include quantitative and qualitative social autopsy assessment.

records.projectTitle: MUST COBERS UGANDA
records.collaborators: Jodi Lori (School of Nursing), Mary Ruffolo (School of Social Work), Gad N. Ruzaaza (Mbarara University of Science and Technology)
records.projectDescription: Embedding UM students from schools of social work, medicine, and nursing in community health and leadership training projects in SW Uganda, alongside MUST students.

records.projectTitle: The Role of Indigenous Plants in Immunity
records.collaborators: Patrick Ogwang, Mbarara University of Science and Technology
records.projectDescription: Complementary medicine is treatments that are used along with standard medical treatments but are not considered to be standard treatments. According to a comprehensive survey, 17.7 % of American adults used complementary health approaches such as a dietary supplement other than vitamins and minerals in the past year. While there are indications that some may be helpful, more needs to be learned about the effects of these products in the human body, and about their safety and potential interactions with medicines and other natural products. In Uganda, a team of scientists led by Dr. Patrick Ogwang showed a high efficacy of anti-malaria effect by Artemisia as a drinking tea product (Artavol). Artemisia is an indigenous plant in Uganda and being used by some communities as herbal tea, taken once a week for malaria prevention. In addition, a limited study showed a beneficial effect of Artavol on HIV patients. The preliminary data obtained my laboratory showed a regulatory effect of Artavol on immune cells. However, it is unknown how Artavol works and thus it is necessary to conduct scientific studies with advanced knowledge and sophisticated technology to have a better understanding of the beneficial effect.
records.fundingSource: External: NIH grant application to be submitted in 2020

records.projectTitle: Integrated Care Systems to Advance Research, Education and Empowerment of Young People Living with HIV (iCARE+)
records.collaborators: Rob Stephenson (UM), Henry Mugerwa (Joint Clinical Research Center - Uganda), Victor Musiime (Joint Clinical Research Center - Uganda), Asia Mbajja (People in-need Agency Uganda), Joseph Mwizerwa (Aga Khan University in Uganda)
records.projectDescription: This project examines the feasibility, acceptability and potential impact of a mobile health (mhealth) intervention to promote self-management of HIV among HIV-infected adolescents and young adults in Uganda

records.projectTitle: Kampala Adolescent and Youth Substance Use
records.collaborators: Aga Khan University School of Nursing in Uganda
records.projectDescription: This project examines the prevalence, patterns, correlates and consequences of substance use and disorders in purposely selected key groups of young people in Uganda. This information will improve our understanding of the key groups most affected by substance use, and provide insights on the factors underlying the observed dynamics of substance use and disorders in this population(s). Taken together, these data will inform facilitate identification of key population and potential strategies to address substance use and disorders among young people in Uganda.
records.fundingDuration: This is a two year project. The goal is to utilize this data to apply for NIH funding

records.projectTitle: Feasibility of Scaling ‘Clean Stacking’ Options in Southern Africa
records.collaborators: Rob Bailis
records.projectDescription: This proposal builds on federally funded research examining the feasibility of alternatives to charcoal in Zambia. The research, supported by the US government's PEER (NAS) and PIRE (NSF) programs, is primarily motivated by concerns about energy poverty and forest degradation resulting from unsustainable woodfuel demand. The research involves quantitative impact evaluations of two private sector led clean cooking dissemination activities intended to dramatically reduce charcoal demand in Lusaka. Vitalite, is promoting "EcoZoom" charcoal stoves. The second, Emerging Cooking Solutions (ECS), is promoting pellet-fueled Mimi Moto stoves. The evaluation will track two groups from each stove promoter, current users (N=300) and potential users (N=600). Because of its verified status as a clean cooking option (discussed in detail below), our ISN activities will focus on ECS's Mimi Moto promotion.

records.projectTitle: PIRE: Confronting Energy Poverty: Building an Interdisciplinary Evidence Base, Network, and Capacity for Transformative Change
records.collaborators: Barbara Entwisle , UNC-Chapel Hill; Michael Emch, UNC-Chapel Hill; Stephen Kelley, North Carolina State University; Andrew Grieshop, North Carolina State University
records.projectDescription: Sub-Saharan Africa is the epicenter of the global challenge of energy poverty, defined as the lack of access to electricity and reliance on biomass fuels. In Southern Africa, the absolute number of energy poor is projected to increase through 2050. Energy poverty has implications for climate, environmental sustainability, human health, and well-being, with negative impacts realized at individual and collective-scales, and in local, regional, and global contexts. The complex socio-environmental challenge of energy poverty requires contributions from engineering and the natural and social sciences, with integration of evidence and learning using robust interdisciplinary frameworks. Through the Energy Poverty PIRE (EPP) we will foster and sustain a network of academic, practitioner, and policy communities to fill critical gaps in the theoretical and empirical evidence base regarding strategies for mitigating energy poverty. International partnership is critical to the identification and study of important novel and scalable energy poverty innovations, analyzing the socio-environmental causes and consequences of energy poverty at multiple-scales, and to developing a virtuous cycle of evidence based policy making. The EPP will train over 70 postdoctoral, graduate and undergraduate researchers from a disciplines as diverse as anthropology and engineering in excellence in interdisciplinary collaborative research in low resource international settings

records.fundingSource: External: Fogarty International Center of the National Institutes of Health

records.projectTitle: Mental Health Needs among Zambian LGBT Communities
records.collaborators: Levy Ngosa, Sarah Chirwa, Besa Chisabga, Musonda Mulopa, & Lawrence Kamuchungu (Friends of Rainka in Zambia); Gabriel Johnson, Laura Jadwin-Cakmak, Jessica Miller, Brandon Bond, & Juan Jauregui (University of Michigan School of Public Health)
records.projectDescription: In Zambia, lesbian, gay, bisexual and transgender (LGBT) people contend with interpersonal, social and structural-level stigma and discrimination due to their sexual orientation and/or gender identity and expression. Such anti-LGBT stigma, discrimination, violence and oppression have been associated with higher reported levels of psychological distress and poor mental health outcomes. In Zambia, multiple forms of stigma may interact with each other to produce a synergistic influence on the mental health of LGBT people in Zambia. Despite this potential for negative mental health outcomes among LGBT people in Zambia, no studies have explored this health inequity. The proposed exploratory research project is designed to generate important preliminary data regarding the mental health concerns experienced by LGBT in Zambia and identify strategies for intervention that are both feasible and culturally appropriate. This mixed-methods (qualitative and quantitative) study is being conducted in collaboration with the largest national LGBT non-governmental organization in Zambia, Friends of Rainka (FOR), and utilize community-based participatory research principles. We have conducted five focus groups (n=35) with LGBT people in Lusaka, Zambia to identify priority mental health issues, understand conceptualizations of mental health and well-being, and identify socioecological influences on mental health/well-being, and mental health services. Currently we are collecting quantitative surveys (n=200) completed by LGBT community members to explore relationships between demographic variables, mental health symptoms, and factors related to psychological risk and resilience.
records.fundingSource: U-M: School of Public Health Office of Global Public Health
records.fundingDuration: Our funding ended in December 2019, but we are continuing the project through volunteer work

records.projectTitle: African Art After Independence, 1957-1977
records.collaborators: Laura De Becker, Kelly Askew, Franc Nunoo-Quarcoo
records.projectDescription: In 1957, Ghana became the first African country to declare independence from its colonizers, spearheading a wave of political uprisings across the continent. By 1970, forty-five of today’s fifty-four African states had regained their independence. The autonomy of these nations coincided with artistic revolutions. Everywhere, artists began rethinking their relationship to the new nation state, to the African continent and to a world fractured by the Cold War. African Art After Independence, 1957-1977 explores how modern African artists were at the forefront of these critical conversations.
records.fundingDuration: U-M funding until August 2020. Additional funding is being sought from the NEA and NEH to support an exhibition and book.

records.projectTitle: Creating a Network for Gender-Based Violence Prevention at Universities across Sub-Saharan Africa
records.collaborators: Sarah Compton - UM Department of OB/GYN; Eugene Darteh - University of Cape Coast, Ghana; Ruth Owusu-Antwi - Kwame Nkrumah University of Science and Technology, Ghana; Sibongile Kamusoko - National University of Science and Technology, Zimbabwe; Sinegugu Duma - University of KwaZulu-Natal, South Africa; Akanni Akinyemi - Obafemi Awolowo University, Nigeria
records.projectDescription: Gender-based violence (GBV), including sexual violence, is a serious problem affecting the safety, dignity, health, and well-being of millions of individuals around the globe, with high rates among university students. Rates of GBV in sub-Saharan Africa are higher than in other regions; South Africa has the highest rate of GBV in the world. There is a recognized need for programs that explicitly address GBV among university students in sub-Saharan Africa. However, currently universities are working independently to address this problem. There is a need for a systematic evaluation and a network to share best practices, lessons learned, and resources. This proposal brings together five universities from western (Ghana, Nigeria), eastern (Zimbabwe), and southern (South Africa) parts of sub-Saharan Africa. The team has research, educational, and administrative experience related to GBV programming on university campuses. Thus, the overall objective of this proposal is to create a GBV research and prevention network to conduct initial research on GBV knowledge, attitudes, and behaviors among university students in sub-Saharan Africa. This will help establish a formal Sub-Saharan University GBV Research and Prevention Network to share needs, resources, and best practices to improve the research environment and strengthen individual and institutional research capabilities across network partners as well as expand to other interested institutions.
records.fundingSource: U-M: pending ASC (ASRI seed grant). External: pending NIH application for Sexual Violence Research Initiative
